docs.title: ScipionWeb Documentation
docs.text:
What is ScipionWeb?

ScipionWeb is a web interface for managing Scipion projects, protocols, outputs, viewers, plugins, and collaboration workflows from the browser.

This documentation helps installers, users, administrators, and developers find the right information without having to understand the whole system first.

docs.text:

docs.title: Recommended deployment mode
docs.text:
Integrated Mode (recommended)Separate frontend/backendAPI-only

The backend serves the compiled frontend and mounts the API under /api.

  • Web UI: http://host:8080/
  • API docs: http://host:8080/api/docs

This is the recommended mode when installing ScipionWeb for users.

Use this when the frontend is hosted separately from the API, usually in more advanced infrastructure setups.

Use this mainly for development, testing, or deployments where another service provides the frontend.

docs.title: Quick install example
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe" \ --web-dist "$HOME/scipionweb/ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip"

docs.title: What ScipionWeb provides
docs.text:
Main capabilities

  • Web-based project and protocol management
  • Output previews and specialized viewers
  • User authentication and sharing workflows
  • Plugin-related workflows
  • PostgreSQL persistence
  • Celery + Redis background task execution
  • Integrated API + Web deployment mode
  • CLI tools for installation, provisioning, runtime control, and logs

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
This page is kept only as a compatibility entry point for older links.

The current architecture documentation lives in the developer reference section:

Open Backend Architecture

docs.title: Backend Overview
docs.text:
ScipionAPI is a FastAPI-based backend that exposes a REST API for:

  • project management
  • protocol execution
  • output previews
  • plugin management
  • authentication and authorization

It is designed to:

  • run inside a Scipion-capable Python environment
  • use PostgreSQL for persistence
  • use Redis for background task brokering
  • support both integrated and distributed deployments

Scope of this section

This section documents the backend architecture and runtime internals. For installation and runtime configuration, see the Installation and Configuration sections.

docs.title: Core Responsibilities
docs.text:
The backend is responsible for:

  • managing users and authentication
  • managing projects and protocols
  • executing background tasks
  • serving output previews
  • integrating with the Scipion runtime
  • persisting metadata in PostgreSQL

A practical mental model is that the backend is the operational center of ScipionWeb: the web UI requests data and actions, but persistence, orchestration, permissions, and asynchronous execution live here.

docs.title: Runtime Structure
docs.text:
Key modules (high-level):

text app/backend/ ├── main.py ├── database.py ├── bootstrap.py ├── api/ │ ├── routers/ │ ├── services/ │ ├── schemas/ │ └── dependencies.py ├── models/ ├── mapper/ ├── utils/

Read this section in order

Start with Architecture, then FastAPI, Auth, Database, and Celery. Finish with Troubleshooting for operational debugging patterns.

docs.title: Startup Behavior (High Level)
docs.text:
At startup, ScipionAPI typically:

  1. loads environment from SCIPION_HOME/.env
  2. initializes the Scipion environment
  3. registers routers
  4. applies middleware and error handlers
  5. starts serving API requests

If background execution is expected, a healthy Celery worker and Redis broker are also part of the effective runtime, even though they are not the same process as the API server.

docs.title: Common backend failure boundaries
docs.text:
Many problems that feel like a single backend failure actually belong to different layers:

  • API starts, but authentication fails
  • API is healthy, but tasks never finish
  • UI loads, but data is inconsistent
  • migrations fail during upgrade

Identifying the layer first usually shortens debugging dramatically.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: CLI Reference         Next: Backend Architecture →

docs.title: Backend Architecture
docs.text:
ScipionAPI follows a layered architecture designed to keep HTTP concerns, business logic, and persistence logic clearly separated.

Core flow:

text Routers → Services → ORM / Mapper → Database

Why this matters

This separation improves maintainability, testability, and feature evolution. Changes in one layer are less likely to leak across the whole backend.

docs.title: Routers
docs.text:
Located in:

text app/backend/api/routers/

Responsibilities:

  • Define HTTP endpoints
  • Validate and parse requests
  • Inject dependencies
  • Return API responses

Routers should remain thin and focused on the HTTP layer.

docs.title: Services
docs.text:
Located in:

text app/backend/api/services/

Responsibilities:

  • Business logic
  • Project operations
  • Protocol management
  • Plugin orchestration
  • Output preview generation

Service boundary

Services should not depend on HTTP-specific details (status codes, request objects, etc.) unless a deliberate design choice requires it.

docs.title: Models (ORM)
docs.text:
Located in:

text app/backend/models/

Technology:

  • SQLAlchemy ORM
  • Declarative models
  • Relationship mapping

Models represent persistent entities and relational structure used by the application.

docs.title: Mapper
docs.text:
The mapper layer provides additional relational operations beyond ORM convenience methods.

Typical uses:

  • Project sharing
  • Custom joins
  • Flat relational queries
  • Query patterns optimized for specific backend workflows

ORM + mapper coexistence

Using both ORM models and mapper utilities is common when some queries are easier to express or maintain outside standard model methods.

docs.title: Request Flow
docs.text:
Typical request path:

text Client → Router → Dependency Injection → Service → DB → Response

docs.title: Expanded interpretation
docs.text:
1. Client sends HTTP request
  2. Router matches endpoint and validates inputs
  3. Dependencies provide user/session/context
  4. Service executes business logic
  5. DB layer persists/reads state
  6. Router returns response payload

docs.title: Dependency Injection
docs.text:
FastAPI dependencies commonly provide:

  • Current user
  • JWT validation
  • Database session

Defined in:

text api/dependencies.py

Strong dependency contracts

Keep dependency helpers focused and reusable (for example auth, DB session, permission checks). This keeps routers consistent and easier to audit.

docs.title: Separation of Concerns
docs.text:
ScipionAPI follows these practical boundaries:

  • Routers do not contain business logic
  • Services do not depend on the HTTP layer
  • Database/persistence does not know about authentication policy details

docs.title: Why this is useful
docs.text:
• Easier testing (unit vs integration)
  • Lower coupling between features
  • Simpler refactors when APIs evolve
  • Cleaner onboarding for new contributors

docs.title: Extensibility Pattern
docs.text:
A new feature typically requires:

  1. New service method
  2. New router endpoint
  3. Optional database migration
  4. Optional background task (Celery)

Feature checklist

When adding features, decide early whether the operation is:

  • synchronous (request/response), or
  • asynchronous (background task via Celery)

That choice affects service design, API semantics, and user feedback flow.

docs.title: Example Change Path
docs.text:
Simple read-only featureState-changing featureHeavy/long-running feature
  1. Add service query method
  2. Add router endpoint
  3. Return serialized response
  1. Add service method
  2. Add router endpoint
  3. Update ORM / mapper logic
  4. Add tests
  5. Add migration if schema changed
  1. Add service entrypoint
  2. Enqueue Celery task
  3. Track task status / logs
  4. Return async-friendly API response

docs.title: Common Architecture Smells
docs.text:
Business logic inside routers

If routers grow large and contain branching domain logic, move that logic into services.

Service methods returning HTTP-specific responses

Returning raw HTTP response objects from services increases coupling to FastAPI/router concerns.

Direct DB usage scattered across routers

Keep persistence access centralized to services/mapper utilities where possible.

Hidden cross-layer dependencies

Be explicit about dependencies (auth, DB session, current user) to avoid brittle side effects.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: Backend Overview         Next: FastAPI App and Routers →

docs.title: Authentication and JWT
docs.text:
ScipionAPI uses JWT-based authentication for API access control.

Algorithm:

text HS256

docs.title: Token Model
docs.text:
Common token concepts:

  • Access token
  • Refresh token (if implemented in your deployment/version)
  • sub claim containing the user email

Version-dependent behavior

Refresh-token support may be optional or evolve over time. Confirm the exact flow implemented in your backend version.

docs.title: Login Flow
docs.text:
Typical authentication flow:

  1. User submits credentials
  2. Password is verified against the stored hash
  3. JWT is generated
  4. Token is returned to the client

docs.title: Authorization Header
docs.text:
Clients must send:

text Authorization: Bearer <token>

This header is typically included on protected API requests.

Common client error

Missing Bearer prefix or malformed headers will cause authentication failures even if the token string itself is valid.

docs.title: JWT Verification
docs.text:
JWT verification is performed in:

text api/dependencies.py

Typical verification steps:

  1. Decode token using SECRET_KEY
  2. Validate signature
  3. Extract sub
  4. Fetch user from database

Dependency-based auth

Performing verification in dependencies keeps router code clean and standardizes auth handling across endpoints.

docs.title: SECRET_KEY
docs.text:
Defined in .env:

dotenv SECRET_KEY=...

Requirements:

  • Strong
  • Private
  • Rotated if compromised

Critical secret

If SECRET_KEY is exposed, existing tokens may become untrusted. Rotate the key and re-issue credentials/tokens according to your security policy.

docs.title: Password Hashing
docs.text:
Passwords are stored hashed (bcrypt).

  • Never stored in plain text
  • Verified by comparing the submitted password against the stored hash

Security hygiene

Keep hashing and verification logic centralized and avoid duplicating password-processing code across modules.

docs.title: Production Recommendations
docs.text:
• Use HTTPS everywhere
  • Restrict token lifetime
  • Rotate SECRET_KEY periodically (planned process)
  • Implement refresh tokens if required by your UX/security model
  • Audit CORS + auth behavior together in browser-based deployments

docs.title: Common Auth Failures
docs.text:
Invalid token

Check token expiry, signature validity, and whether the token was generated with the current SECRET_KEY.

User not found for token sub

The token may decode correctly, but the referenced user no longer exists (or the sub claim format changed).

Works in API client, fails in browser

This is often a CORS/header issue rather than a JWT issue. Confirm the browser is actually sending Authorization.

Token generated but endpoints still reject access

Verify the protected dependency chain and role/permission checks used by the target endpoint.

docs.title: Debugging Checklist
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check runtime health first
docs.text: curl http://localhost:8080/health

docs.title: Then inspect backend logs
docs.text:
./scripts/scipionapi logs ```

Inspect:

  • auth-related exceptions
  • header parsing errors
  • JWT decode/validation failures
  • user lookup failures

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: FastAPI App and Routers         Next: Database and Alembic Migrations →

docs.title: Celery and Redis
docs.text:
ScipionAPI uses Celery for background task execution.

Redis acts as:

  • Broker
  • Result backend

This enables the backend to offload heavy or long-running work from the request/response path.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Defined in .env:

dotenv BROKER_URL=redis://localhost:6379/0

Redis role

In this backend architecture, Redis is used by Celery infrastructure and must be available for task dispatching and processing.

docs.title: Worker Entry Point
docs.text:
Celery worker entry point:

text app/workers/task_queue.py

This module defines/loads the Celery app and task registration used by the worker process.

docs.title: Via CLI (recommended for local usage)
docs.text:
bash ./scripts/scipionapi start

This typically starts both:

  • FastAPI/uvicorn
  • Celery worker

docs.title: Manual worker start (advanced)
docs.text:
bash celery -A app.workers.task_queue worker --loglevel=info

Manual mode

Manual Celery startup is useful for debugging worker behavior independently from API startup.

docs.title: Typical Use Cases
docs.text:
Background tasks are useful for:

  • Plugin installation
  • Heavy computation tasks
  • Long-running background jobs
  • Operations that should not block API responses

docs.title: Task Flow
docs.text:
High-level task lifecycle:

text API → Celery Broker (Redis) → Worker → Redis (result/state) → API / client polling

Response model

Depending on the API endpoint design, clients may receive immediate acknowledgement and then track progress/status asynchronously.

docs.title: Failure Handling
docs.text:
Operational behavior typically includes:

  • Configurable retries (task-dependent)
  • Error logging in worker logs (for example celery.log)
  • Redis connectivity dependency for enqueue/processing

Silent queueing assumptions

If the API is healthy but work is not progressing, confirm the worker is running and Redis is reachable before debugging task code.

docs.title: Production Recommendations
docs.text:
• Run the worker via systemd
  • Monitor Redis health and memory usage
  • Configure log rotation for worker logs
  • Track worker restarts/failures
  • Separate API and worker service supervision

Observability matters

Background-task failures are often operationally invisible to users until they inspect logs. Prioritize log visibility and service monitoring.

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Tasks are not processed

Check that the worker is running and connected to the same Redis instance configured in BROKER_URL.

Worker starts but crashes

Inspect dependency imports, runtime environment activation, and backend logs (celery.log).

API enqueues task but no result appears

Verify Redis connectivity, task registration, and result backend behavior.

Works locally, fails in production

Compare .env, systemd service environment, and filesystem permissions between environments.

docs.title: Quick Verification
docs.text:
bash ./scripts/scipionapi status ./scripts/scipionapi logs sudo systemctl status redis-server

Look for:

  • worker process running
  • Redis healthy
  • task exceptions in celery.log

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: Database and Alembic Migrations         Next: Backend Troubleshooting →

docs.title: Database and Alembic Migrations
docs.text:
ScipionAPI persistence is built around:

  • PostgreSQL
  • SQLAlchemy ORM
  • Alembic migrations

This combination supports relational data modeling, schema evolution, and predictable deployment upgrades.

docs.title: Database URL
docs.text:
Defined in .env:

dotenv DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@host:5432/db

Keep it consistent

If your deployment also uses split DB variables (DATABASE_NAME, DATABASE_USER, DATABASE_PASS), keep them aligned with DATABASE_URL.

docs.title: ORM Models
docs.text:
Models are located in:

text app/backend/models/

Typical entities include:

  • User
  • Project
  • Protocol
  • ProjectShare
  • Settings
  • Tags

Model set may evolve

The exact list of models may change as features are added (for example new metadata or sharing/tagging entities).

docs.title: Alembic
docs.text:
Migration files are stored in:

text alembic/

Alembic tracks schema revisions and applies them in order.

docs.title: Apply Migrations
docs.text:
Run:

bash alembic upgrade head

This upgrades the database schema to the latest known revision.

Before production migrations

Always back up the database before applying migrations in production.

docs.title: Create a New Migration
docs.text:
Generate a migration:

bash alembic revision --autogenerate -m "description"

Then review the generated migration carefully before applying it.

Autogenerate is not infallible

Always inspect generated migrations for unexpected drops, type changes, defaults, or constraint operations.

docs.title: Migration Flow
docs.text:
text Model change → alembic revision → review migration → alembic upgrade

Recommended workflow:

  1. Update ORM models
  2. Generate migration
  3. Review/edit migration if needed
  4. Test on a local/staging DB
  5. Apply to production (after backup)

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Never edit already applied migrations in shared environments
  • Test migrations on staging before production
  • Back up the database before production migration
  • Keep migration history linear and understandable when possible
  • Review SQL/DDL implications for large tables

docs.title: Troubleshooting Migration State
docs.text:
If migration state becomes inconsistent:

  • Use a fresh empty database to isolate schema issues
  • Inspect the alembic_version table
  • Confirm all migration files are present and in the expected order
  • Check whether any applied migration was modified after deployment

Modified migration files

Editing an already applied migration can cause hard-to-debug state mismatches across environments.

docs.title: Operational Checks
docs.text:
Verify connectivity before migrations:

bash psql -U user -d db

Then run:

bash alembic upgrade head

After migration, validate expected tables:

bash psql -U user -d db -c "\dt"

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: Authentication and JWT         Next: Celery and Redis →

docs.title: FastAPI App and Routers
docs.text:
The FastAPI application is defined in:

text app/backend/main.py

This module is responsible for application initialization, middleware/error setup, router registration, and exposing the API runtime.

docs.title: Application Initialization
docs.text:
Main startup steps typically include:

  1. Load environment via bootstrapEnv()
  2. Create the FastAPI app instance
  3. Register error handlers
  4. Configure CORS
  5. Prepare the Scipion runtime
  6. Include routers

Startup sequence

Exact ordering can vary slightly by version, but environment loading and core app initialization should happen before router usage.

docs.title: CORS Configuration
docs.text:
CORS is defined in main.py.

Example (development):

python allow_origins = [ "http://localhost:5173", "http://localhost:5174" ]

Production guidance:

  • Restrict origins to trusted frontend URLs
  • Use HTTPS
  • Avoid permissive wildcard origins unless strictly required for internal scenarios

Common production issue

Browser requests can fail due to CORS even when the API is healthy. Verify the exact frontend origin (scheme + host + port).

docs.title: Router Modules
docs.text:
Router modules are typically located in:

text app/backend/api/routers/

Common router groups include:

  • project_router
  • protocol_router
  • plugin_router
  • auth_router
  • user_router

Router organization

Group endpoints by domain responsibility (projects, protocols, plugins, auth, users) to keep files focused and discoverable.

docs.title: Example Router Pattern
docs.text:
python @router.get("/projects") def listProjects(): return service.getProjects()

This illustrates the desired pattern:

  • router defines HTTP endpoint
  • service handles business logic

Naming style

Function naming conventions may vary across the codebase. Keep naming consistent within each module and favor readability.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Custom error handlers are registered via:

text registerAllErrorHandlers(app)

Typical handled categories:

  • Validation errors
  • Authentication errors
  • Internal errors

docs.title: Why centralized handlers help
docs.text:
• Consistent API error responses
  • Cleaner router code
  • Easier logging and debugging
  • Better client-side integration

docs.title: OpenAPI Docs
docs.text:
FastAPI documentation is available at:

text /docs

In integrated mode, it is commonly available at:

text /api/docs

(actual final path depends on API mount configuration)

Integrated mode reminder

If the API is mounted under /api, use /api/docs instead of /docs.

docs.title: Health Endpoint
docs.text:
Health endpoint:

text GET /health

Expected response:

json {"status":"ok"}

Use it for:

  • runtime validation after startup
  • monitoring checks
  • deployment verification
  • quick troubleshooting

docs.title: Operational Checks
docs.text:
After starting services:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi status curl http://localhost:8080/health

If requests fail in browser but curl works, inspect:

  • CORS configuration
  • reverse proxy path rewrites
  • frontend API base URL settings

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Routers not reachable

Check that router modules are included in main.py and that the expected prefix/mount path is configured.

Docs route changed

In integrated mode or reverse-proxied setups, docs may be under /api/docs instead of /docs.

CORS errors in frontend only

Confirm allow_origins matches the real frontend origin exactly (scheme, domain, port).

Startup succeeds but runtime fails later

Validate database/Redis connectivity and inspect logs/app.log.

docs.text: ← Previous: Backend Architecture         Next: Authentication and JWT →

docs.title: Backend Troubleshooting
docs.text:
This page collects common backend issues and a practical debugging workflow for ScipionAPI.

Start with the basics

Most runtime issues are caused by one of these categories:

  • wrong Conda environment
  • invalid .env values
  • database connectivity problems
  • Redis / Celery runtime issues
  • CORS or auth misconfiguration

docs.title: Quick Triage Checklist
docs.text:
Before deep debugging:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi status ./scripts/scipionapi logs curl http://localhost:8080/health

Then verify:

  • SCIPION_HOME points to the expected runtime workspace
  • .env exists and has the expected values
  • PostgreSQL and Redis are reachable

docs.title: Cause
docs.text: • Not running inside the correct Conda environment

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
bash conda activate scipion4Web

If using the wrapper script, re-run commands from the ScipionAPI bundle directory:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi status

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL is running
  • DATABASE_URL is correct
  • Database role exists
  • Network/firewall access is correct (remote DB setups)

docs.title: Test manually
docs.text:
bash psql -U user -d db

Credential mismatches

If .env was changed recently, ensure the running service was restarted and is not using stale environment values.

docs.title: Possible causes
docs.text:
• Inconsistent migration history
  • Missing tables
  • Modified migration files
  • Wrong target database

docs.title: Debug steps
docs.text:
• Test on a fresh empty DB
  • Inspect alembic_version
  • Confirm migration files exist and match the deployed version

docs.title: 4. Redis Connection Error
docs.text:
Check Redis service status:

bash sudo systemctl status redis-server

Also verify the broker URL in .env:

dotenv BROKER_URL=redis://localhost:6379/0

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• Worker is running
  • Redis is reachable
  • Worker logs (celery.log) show task receipt/execution
  • BROKER_URL matches between API and worker runtime

docs.title: Useful commands
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi status ./scripts/scipionapi logs

docs.title: Ensure
docs.text:
• Correct allow_origins in FastAPI app
  • Proper frontend URL (scheme + host + port)
  • Reverse proxy is not rewriting origins unexpectedly

Browser-only failures

If API requests work with curl but fail in the browser, CORS is a likely cause.

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• SECRET_KEY matches the token-signing key
  • Token is not expired
  • Authorization header uses Bearer <token>
  • User referenced by token sub still exists

docs.title: General Debugging Workflow
docs.text:
Enable verbose logging in development when possible, then inspect:

  • logs/app.log
  • logs/celery.log

Recommended sequence:

  1. Stop services (if state is unclear)
  2. Check logs
  3. Validate .env
  4. Validate DB connectivity
  5. Validate Redis connectivity
  6. Restart services
  7. Re-test /health and failing endpoint

docs.title: When in Doubt (Recovery Loop)
docs.text:
bash ./scripts/scipionapi stop ./scripts/scipionapi logs ./scripts/scipionapi start ./scripts/scipionapi status curl http://localhost:8080/health

If the problem persists, isolate by subsystem:

  • API startup only
  • DB connectivity only
  • Redis/Celery only
  • Auth/CORS path only

docs.title: Escalation Tips for Teams
docs.text:
When reporting backend issues to teammates, include:

  • exact command run
  • error message (full traceback if available)
  • relevant .env keys (redacted secrets)
  • status output
  • recent app.log / celery.log lines
  • whether the issue reproduces on a fresh DB or staging

Faster debugging

A good bug report often saves more time than a quick patch attempt without logs.

docs.title: CLI Reference Overview
docs.text:
ScipionAPI provides a command-line interface (CLI) to manage the full lifecycle of a deployment:

  • environment bootstrapping
  • runtime and database installation
  • one-shot provisioning
  • service lifecycle management
  • logs and runtime status inspection

The main entrypoint is the wrapper script:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi

If the package is already available inside the active environment, you may also use:

bash scipionapi

Recommended usage

For most users, the wrapper script (./scripts/scipionapi) is the safest option because it helps ensure the command runs in the expected Conda environment.

docs.title: How to think about the CLI
docs.text:
A simple mental model is:

  • bootstrap prepares Python and dependencies
  • install prepares runtime configuration and database state
  • provision combines setup into one guided path
  • start, stop, restart, status, and logs manage the running services

If you remember that split, the CLI becomes much easier to understand.

docs.title: Command Groups
docs.text:
Category Commands Purpose Environment bootstrap Create or refresh the Conda runtime and install Python dependencies Installation install, provision Configure runtime workspace, database, admin user, and optional web deployment Runtime start, stop, restart, status, logs Manage API/Celery processes and inspect runtime logs

Fastest path

If you want a complete setup in one command, use provision.

docs.title: Available Commands
docs.text:
• bootstrap
  • install
  • provision
  • start
  • stop
  • restart
  • status
  • logs

docs.title: Typical Workflows
docs.text:
Recommended (one-shot setup)Step-by-step (advanced or debugging)Daily runtime operations

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe"

bash ./scripts/scipionapi bootstrap ./scripts/scipionapi install --user "admin" --email "admin@example.com" --pass "changeMe" ./scripts/scipionapi start

bash ./scripts/scipionapi status ./scripts/scipionapi logs ./scripts/scipionapi restart

docs.title: Execution Model
docs.text:
The wrapper script typically:

  1. detects or prepares the Conda environment
  2. runs the CLI inside that environment
  3. ensures Python dependencies are available for execution

That is why using the wrapper is often more reliable than invoking scattered commands manually.

docs.title: First-time install
docs.text:
1. provision
  2. runtime commands
  3. logs and PID files

docs.title: Manual or debugging-oriented setup
docs.text:
1. bootstrap
  2. install
  3. runtime commands
  4. Backend Troubleshooting

docs.title: General help
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi --help

docs.title: Command-specific help
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision --help ./scripts/scipionapi install --help ./scripts/scipionapi start --help

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
Mixing provision and partial manual steps without checking state

provision is idempotent in common cases, but always confirm your runtime paths and database target before re-running commands.

Running commands outside the expected project directory

Use the wrapper from the API bundle directory so relative paths resolve correctly.

Forgetting to inspect logs after startup

A service can start and still fail shortly after. Use status and logs to verify runtime health.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: Configuration Overview         Next: bootstrap →

docs.title: bootstrap Command
docs.text:
The bootstrap command creates and prepares the Conda environment used by ScipionAPI.

It prepares the Python runtime and dependencies, but it does not perform installation tasks such as database creation or migrations.

docs.title: What bootstrap does not do
docs.text:
• It does not create the database
  • It does not run Alembic migrations
  • It does not start services

Scope

bootstrap focuses only on preparing the Python execution environment.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi bootstrap

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
When executed, bootstrap typically:

  1. Checks whether Conda is available
  2. Creates the environment (default: scipion4Web) if it does not exist
  3. Installs the Python version (default: 3.8)
  4. Upgrades pip
  5. Installs dependencies from requirements.txt
  6. Installs the package in editable mode (pip install -e .)

docs.title: Default Environment Name
docs.text:
Default Conda environment name:

text scipion4Web

This may be overridden in advanced setups (wrapper/env config, depending on version/workflow).

docs.title: When to Use bootstrap
docs.text:
Use bootstrap when you need to:

  • Prepare a development environment
  • Debug Conda or dependency issues
  • Rebuild or refresh the Python runtime
  • Perform a manual installation flow before install
Manual / advanced flowOne-shot alternative

bash ./scripts/scipionapi bootstrap ./scripts/scipionapi install --user "admin" --email "admin@example.com" --pass "changeMe" ./scripts/scipionapi start

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision --user "admin" --email "admin@example.com" --pass "changeMe"

docs.title: Idempotent Behavior
docs.text:
Running bootstrap multiple times is generally safe.

Typical behavior:

  • Does not recreate the environment if it already exists
  • Reinstalls or refreshes dependencies if needed

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
bash ./scripts/scipionapi bootstrap

After completion:

bash conda activate scipion4Web scipionapi --help

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Conda not found

Confirm Conda is installed and available in PATH:

bash conda --version

Environment created but command still fails

Activate the environment manually and test:

bash conda activate scipion4Web scipionapi --help

Dependency installation errors

Re-run bootstrap and inspect terminal output. If the problem persists, verify Conda/Python compatibility and network access for package downloads.

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text:
After bootstrap, continue with:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi install --user "admin" --email "admin@example.com" --pass "changeMe"

Or use the one-shot alternative:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision --user "admin" --email "admin@example.com" --pass "changeMe"

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: CLI Reference Overview         Next: install →

docs.title: install Command
docs.text:
The install command configures the runtime workspace and the database for ScipionAPI.

It prepares the application to run, but it does not start runtime services.

Scope

install handles runtime configuration, database setup, migrations, and admin-user creation. Service startup is a separate step unless you use provision.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi install \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe"

docs.title: Mental model
docs.text:
Use install when:

  • Python and dependencies are already prepared
  • you want controlled installation steps
  • you need to inspect database and runtime setup before starting services

A simple sequence is:

  1. bootstrap
  2. install
  3. start

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
install typically performs the following actions:

  1. creates or updates SCIPION_HOME
  2. generates .env
  3. creates required folders such as logs/ and projects/
  4. creates the PostgreSQL role and database in common local setups
  5. runs alembic upgrade head
  6. creates or updates the admin user

docs.title: Automatic local database bootstrap
docs.text: If PostgreSQL is local and the user has sudo privileges, the role and database can usually be created automatically.

docs.title: Remote PostgreSQL
docs.text:
If you use a remote PostgreSQL server:

  • create the database and role manually
  • ensure .env contains a valid DATABASE_URL
  • confirm connectivity before running install

docs.title: What to do next
docs.text:
After install, continue with:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi start ./scripts/scipionapi status curl http://localhost:8080/health

docs.title: Idempotent Behavior
docs.text:
Running install again is generally safe.

Typical behavior:

  • does not drop the database
  • updates admin credentials if needed
  • ensures migrations are applied and up to date

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
PostgreSQL authentication failed

Verify database credentials in .env and confirm the PostgreSQL role exists with the expected password.

Alembic migration error

Check connectivity and migration history state. A fresh empty database can help isolate migration problems.

Admin user not updated as expected

Re-run install with explicit options and inspect output or logs for validation errors.

docs.title: provision Command
docs.text:
The provision command performs a complete one-shot installation of ScipionAPI.

It combines:

  • bootstrap
  • install
  • optional Web bundle deployment
  • runtime service startup

Recommended for most users

provision is the fastest and most convenient way to get a working installation.

docs.title: Usage (API-only mode)
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe"

docs.title: Usage (Integrated mode: API + Web)
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe" \ --web-dist /path/to/ScipionWeb-dist.zip

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --user Admin username --email Admin email --pass Admin password --web-dist Path to compiled Web bundle (.zip) or extracted dist/ folder --api-mount-path API mount path (default: /api) --api-base-url API base URL used by the frontend

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
provision typically performs the following steps:

  1. Bootstraps the Conda environment (if needed)
  2. Installs Python dependencies
  3. Configures the runtime workspace (SCIPION_HOME)
  4. Creates the database/role (local PostgreSQL scenarios)
  5. Runs Alembic migrations
  6. Creates or updates the admin user
  7. Deploys the Web bundle (optional)
  8. Starts the API and Celery worker

docs.title: API-only mode
docs.text: text http://localhost:8080/docs

docs.title: Integrated mode
docs.text: text http://localhost:8080/ http://localhost:8080/api/docs

docs.title: Re-running provision
docs.text:
provision is generally safe to run multiple times.

Typical behavior:

  • Does not destroy the database
  • Reapplies migrations (as needed)
  • Updates admin credentials
  • Replaces Web assets if --web-dist is provided again

Be intentional in production

Even idempotent commands should be run carefully in production. Confirm target paths, database endpoints, and Web bundle version before re-running.

docs.title: Common Usage Patterns
docs.text:
First-time local setupIntegrated mode with frontend bundleCustom API mount path

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe"

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe" \ --web-dist "$HOME/scipionweb/ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip"

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe" \ --web-dist /path/to/ScipionWeb-dist.zip \ --api-mount-path /api \ --api-base-url /api

docs.title: Verification After Provision
docs.text:
bash ./scripts/scipionapi status curl http://localhost:8080/health

Expected response:

json {"status":"ok"}

Then open:

  • API-only mode: http://localhost:8080/docs
  • Integrated mode: http://localhost:8080/ and http://localhost:8080/api/docs

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: install         Next: runtime commands →

docs.title: Runtime Commands (start, stop, restart, status, logs)
docs.text:
These commands manage ScipionAPI runtime services.

They control:

  • FastAPI / uvicorn
  • Celery worker

Services are typically started as detached background processes when using the CLI runtime commands.

Production recommendation

For production deployments, prefer systemd instead of CLI-managed background processes.

docs.title: Command Summary
docs.text: Command Purpose start Start API and Celery stop Stop API and Celery restart Restart both services status Show whether services are running logs Tail runtime logs

docs.title: Recommended mental model
docs.text:
A practical sequence is:

  1. start to launch services
  2. status to confirm process state
  3. logs to verify real runtime behavior
  4. restart after configuration changes
  5. stop when shutting down intentionally

status tells you whether processes exist. logs and /health tell you whether the system is actually behaving well.

docs.title: start
docs.text:
Starts the API and Celery worker.

bash ./scripts/scipionapi start

Typical behavior:

  • launches API and Celery as detached processes
  • creates PID files under .run/

docs.title: stop
docs.text:
Stops API and Celery.

bash ./scripts/scipionapi stop

Typical behavior:

  • reads PID files
  • terminates the running processes when possible

docs.title: restart
docs.text:
Stops and starts services again.

bash ./scripts/scipionapi restart

Useful after:

  • .env changes
  • dependency or runtime updates
  • integrated Web asset redeployments
  • transient failures

docs.title: status
docs.text:
Shows whether services are running.

bash ./scripts/scipionapi status

Process state vs health

status confirms process state, but not necessarily application health. Also check logs and /health.

docs.title: logs
docs.text:
Tails application logs.

bash ./scripts/scipionapi logs

This typically shows the API log and the worker log together.

docs.title: Common issues
docs.text:
API starts but exits quickly

Check logs and confirm .env settings such as database, Redis, and paths are valid.

Celery not running

Verify Redis availability and broker configuration.

status says running but requests fail

Test runtime health explicitly with /health and inspect logs.

No logs shown

Confirm the configured logs path exists and the runtime user has write permissions.

docs.title: Configuration Overview
docs.text:
This section explains how ScipionWeb is configured at runtime and how configuration is organized across the system, runtime workspace, and application layers.

ScipionWeb configuration is centered around:

  • SCIPION_HOME
  • the .env file
  • deployment mode (API-only, integrated, or separate)
  • runtime environment variables and service connectivity

The configuration model is designed to:

  • keep runtime data separate from source code
  • allow flexible deployment (single host or distributed)
  • support production environments
  • enable reproducible installations and upgrades

What this section covers

These pages focus on runtime configuration after installation. For installation and upgrade steps, see the Installation section.

docs.title: Configuration Layers
docs.text:
ScipionWeb configuration operates at three levels:

  1. System level (Conda, PostgreSQL, Redis, OS services)
  2. Runtime level (SCIPION_HOME)
  3. Application level (.env variables and deployment mode)

Think in layers

When troubleshooting, identify the failing layer first:

  • service availability (system)
  • paths and workspace permissions (runtime)
  • environment variables and endpoints (application)

docs.title: SCIPION_HOME
docs.text:
SCIPION_HOME is the runtime workspace directory.

It stores persistent runtime data and configuration, typically including:

  • .env
  • logs/
  • projects/
  • config/
  • web/ (if using integrated mode)

Why this matters

Keeping SCIPION_HOME separate from the API source or bundle directory makes upgrades easier and helps preserve data across version changes.

docs.title: .env
docs.text:
The .env file is the main runtime configuration file.

It typically defines:

  • database connection settings
  • Redis and broker configuration
  • API host and port
  • paths (logs, projects, web assets)
  • security-related values such as SECRET_KEY
  • deployment behavior flags such as integrated mode and mount paths

Protect your .env

Do not commit .env to version control. It may contain credentials and security-sensitive settings.

docs.title: Deployment Mode
docs.text:
ScipionWeb supports multiple deployment modes:

  • API-only mode
  • Integrated mode (API serves the Web UI)
  • Separate deployment (API and Web UI on different hosts or services)

Each mode changes how paths, URLs, and routing should be configured.

Choose based on your environment

  • Integrated mode is convenient for single-host deployments
  • Separate deployment is common for production setups with independent frontend hosting

docs.title: Default Behavior (Typical Local Setup)
docs.text:
If not overridden, a typical local installation uses:

  • SCIPION_HOME=<repoRoot>/scipion_home
  • API bound to 0.0.0.0:8080
  • Redis at localhost:6379
  • PostgreSQL at localhost:5432

Defaults are a starting point

These defaults are convenient for local setups, but production deployments usually override at least paths, host exposure, and reverse-proxy behavior.

docs.title: 1. SCIPION_HOME and Runtime Layout
docs.text:
Learn how the runtime workspace is structured and what should live inside SCIPION_HOME.

  • runtime directories and responsibilities
  • path conventions
  • permissions and persistence recommendations

➡️ Open SCIPION_HOME and Runtime Layout

docs.title: 2. Environment Variables (.env)
docs.text:
Detailed reference for .env configuration values.

  • database settings
  • API host and port
  • Redis and broker settings
  • paths and runtime behavior
  • security-sensitive values

➡️ Open Environment Variables (.env)

docs.title: 3. API + Web Integrated Mode
docs.text:
Configuration for serving the compiled Web UI from the API process.

  • SERVE_WEB
  • WEB_DIST_PATH
  • API mount path and base URL alignment
  • routing expectations

➡️ Open API + Web Integrated Mode

docs.title: 4. Separate Deployment (API and Web on Different Hosts)
docs.text:
Configuration guidance for decoupled frontend and backend deployments.

  • cross-origin considerations
  • API base URL setup
  • reverse proxy and public URL alignment
  • production deployment patterns

➡️ Open Separate Deployment

docs.text:

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• never commit .env to version control
  • keep SCIPION_HOME outside tracked source files when possible
  • use a strong SECRET_KEY
  • use HTTPS in production
  • use a reverse proxy for public deployments
  • back up runtime configuration before upgrades, especially .env
  • keep API and Web versions aligned when possible

Common source of issues

Many runtime errors come from mismatched paths and URLs, for example WEB_API_BASE_URL, API mount path, reverse proxy routes, or stale SCIPION_HOME values after upgrades.

docs.title: Common configuration smells
docs.text:
Watch for these patterns:

  • .env values that still point to an older installation path
  • frontend assets deployed correctly but API base URL still wrong
  • services running, but under a different SCIPION_HOME than expected
  • local defaults accidentally reused in production

When behavior feels inconsistent, check paths and URLs before assuming code is broken.

← Previous: Installation Overview Next: SCIPION_HOME and Runtime Layout →

docs.title: Environment Variables (.env)
docs.text:
The .env file is the central runtime configuration file for ScipionAPI.

It is typically stored at:

text SCIPION_HOME/.env

This file defines how the API connects to PostgreSQL and Redis, where runtime data is stored, how the API is exposed, and whether the Web UI is served in integrated mode.

Sensitive configuration

The .env file may contain credentials, secrets, and service endpoints. Do not commit it to version control, and protect file permissions in production.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The .env file commonly contains configuration for:

  • PostgreSQL database connection
  • Security settings (SECRET_KEY)
  • API runtime host and port
  • Redis / Celery broker settings
  • Filesystem paths (logs, projects)
  • Integrated mode (API serving the Web UI)
  • Conda integration (auto-detected during provisioning)
  • Admin bootstrap values (used during install/provision workflows)

Version-dependent variables

Some variables may be optional or version-specific. The exact set of supported keys can evolve across releases.

docs.title: File Location
docs.text:
Expected location:

text SCIPION_HOME/.env

Example:

text /opt/scipionweb/runtime/.env

Runtime separation

Keeping .env inside SCIPION_HOME (instead of the API bundle directory) makes upgrades safer and helps preserve configuration across bundle replacements.

docs.title: Core Database Variables
docs.text:
These variables configure the PostgreSQL connection.

dotenv DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:password@host:5432/database DATABASE_NAME=scipion_db DATABASE_USER=scipion_user DATABASE_PASS=securePassword

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• DATABASE_URL is typically the authoritative connection string
  • DATABASE_NAME, DATABASE_USER, and DATABASE_PASS are useful for tooling, bootstrap logic, and operational scripts (depending on workflow)

Recommended practice

Keep the values consistent. A mismatch between DATABASE_URL and the split variables can cause confusing connection or bootstrap errors.

docs.title: SECRET_KEY
docs.text:
dotenv SECRET_KEY=generate_a_long_random_string

Used for:

  • JWT token signing
  • Authentication integrity

Requirements:

  • Strong
  • Private
  • Rotated when needed (especially in production environments with security requirements)

Do not reuse weak secrets

Avoid short or guessable values (for example secret, 123456, or repository placeholders).

docs.title: API Runtime
docs.text:
These variables define where the API listens.

dotenv API_HOST=0.0.0.0 API_PORT=8080

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• 0.0.0.0 binds the API to all network interfaces
  • Change API_PORT if 8080 is already in use
  • If using a reverse proxy (nginx), you may still keep the API on an internal port such as 8080

Production exposure

For public deployments, prefer exposing the service through a reverse proxy with HTTPS instead of exposing the API directly on the internet.

docs.title: Celery / Redis
docs.text:
These variables configure the Celery broker/runtime behavior.

dotenv BROKER_URL=redis://localhost:6379/0 CELERY_LOGLEVEL=info

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• BROKER_URL points to the Redis instance used by Celery
  • CELERY_LOGLEVEL controls worker log verbosity (for example info, warning, debug)

Redis availability

If Redis is down or unreachable, background task processing will fail even if the API starts successfully.

docs.title: Filesystem Paths
docs.text:
These variables define runtime storage locations used by ScipionAPI.

dotenv LOGS_PATH=scipion_home/logs PROJECTS_PATH=scipion_home/projects

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Paths may be relative (depending on how your runtime is structured) or absolute
  • Ensure the service user has write permissions to these locations

Production recommendation

Use predictable, persistent paths and monitor disk usage—especially for PROJECTS_PATH, which can grow significantly over time.

docs.title: Integrated Mode Flags
docs.text:
These variables are used when the API also serves the compiled Web UI (integrated mode).

dotenv SERVE_WEB=1 API_MOUNT_PATH=/api WEB_DIST_PATH=/absolute/path/to/dist WEB_API_BASE_URL=/api

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
• SERVE_WEB=1 enables integrated mode
  • SERVE_WEB=0 (or unset, depending on defaults) keeps the deployment in API-only mode
  • WEB_DIST_PATH points to the extracted frontend dist/ directory (or deployed web assets location)
  • API_MOUNT_PATH and WEB_API_BASE_URL should usually match (for example both /api)

Routing mismatches

If API_MOUNT_PATH and WEB_API_BASE_URL do not align, the frontend may load but API calls can fail due to incorrect routes.

docs.title: Conda Integration
docs.text:
Provisioning and install workflows may auto-detect Conda and store related variables.

dotenv CONDA_EXE=/home/user/miniconda3/bin/conda CONDA_ACTIVATION_CMD=...

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• These values may be auto-generated during provisioning
  • Exact values can differ depending on shell, Conda installation path, and execution environment

Do not guess activation commands

If you modify Conda-related variables manually, test the CLI workflow afterwards (status, logs, restart, etc.).

docs.title: Admin Bootstrap
docs.text:
Some workflows use admin bootstrap values during installation/provisioning.

dotenv ADMIN_USERNAME=admin ADMIN_EMAIL=admin@example.com ADMIN_PASSWORD=...

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Used during install/provision bootstrap workflows
  • May be read to create or update the initial admin user (depending on command and version)

Credentials handling

Do not keep placeholder passwords in production. If these values are used, protect .env permissions and rotate credentials after initial setup when appropriate.

docs.title: Example .env (Local / Integrated Mode)
docs.text:
This is an example composition using common values:

```dotenv DATABASE_URL=postgresql://scipion_user:securePassword@localhost:5432/scipion_db DATABASE_NAME=scipion_db DATABASE_USER=scipion_user DATABASE_PASS=securePassword

SECRET_KEY=replace_with_a_strong_random_secret

API_HOST=0.0.0.0 API_PORT=8080

BROKER_URL=redis://localhost:6379/0 CELERY_LOGLEVEL=info

LOGS_PATH=scipion_home/logs PROJECTS_PATH=scipion_home/projects

SERVE_WEB=1 API_MOUNT_PATH=/api WEB_DIST_PATH=/opt/scipionweb/runtime/web/dist WEB_API_BASE_URL=/api ```

Keep examples separate from real secrets

Use documentation examples or template files for onboarding, but never store real production credentials in shared examples.

docs.title: Loading .env in Shell Sessions (Optional)
docs.text:
If you need to export .env variables into your current shell session for CLI tools:

bash set -a source "$SCIPION_HOME/.env" set +a

Why set -a?

Many .env files use KEY=value format without export. set -a automatically exports variables loaded via source.

docs.title: Production Recommendations
docs.text:
• Do not expose .env
  • Restrict file permissions (chmod 600 or similarly restrictive, depending on deployment)
  • Prefer HTTPS and a reverse proxy for public deployments
  • Back up .env before upgrades
  • Keep database values consistent across DATABASE_URL and split fields
  • Review integrated mode paths after Web/API upgrades

Permission example

A common production setup is:

bash chmod 600 "$SCIPION_HOME/.env" chown <service-user>:<service-user> "$SCIPION_HOME/.env"

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
Stale WEB_DIST_PATH

After upgrading or moving the installation, WEB_DIST_PATH may still point to an old location.

Wrong database host/port

If PostgreSQL runs on another host or non-default port, DATABASE_URL must be updated accordingly.

Relative paths in unexpected working directories

Relative path values may behave differently depending on how the service is started (CLI, systemd, container, etc.).

Mismatched API base path

If you proxy the API under /api, confirm API_MOUNT_PATH and WEB_API_BASE_URL are set consistently.

docs.title: Quick Verification
docs.text:
Run a quick sanity check after editing .env:

bash test -f "$SCIPION_HOME/.env" && echo ".env found" grep -E '^(DATABASE_URL|API_HOST|API_PORT|BROKER_URL|SERVE_WEB)=' "$SCIPION_HOME/.env" || true

Then verify the runtime:

bash curl http://localhost:8080/health

Expected response:

json {"status":"ok"}

← Previous: SCIPION_HOME and Runtime Layout Next: API + Web Integrated Mode →

docs.title: API + Web Integrated Mode
docs.text:
Integrated mode allows ScipionAPI to serve the compiled ScipionWeb frontend directly, creating a single deployment unit.

In this model, the API process serves both:

  • The Web UI (static frontend assets)
  • The REST API
  • API documentation endpoints

Typical use case

Integrated mode is often the easiest option for single-host deployments, local installations, and internal lab servers where simplicity is more important than independent frontend scaling.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
In integrated mode:

  • The Web UI is served at /
  • The API is mounted under /api
  • API docs are available under /api/docs

Typical local URLs:

text http://localhost:8080/ http://localhost:8080/api/docs

Single entry point

Users access one server and one base URL, which simplifies routing, networking, and operations.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Integrated mode is typically enabled during provisioning by passing the compiled Web bundle ZIP:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe" \ --web-dist /path/to/web-dist.zip

During provisioning, the installer typically:

  1. Extracts the Web dist bundle
  2. Copies/deploys it into SCIPION_HOME/web/dist
  3. Enables integrated mode (for example SERVE_WEB=1)
  4. Mounts the API under /api

Deployment path

The exact deployed location can vary by version/workflow, but integrated mode generally uses a runtime path under SCIPION_HOME to store the frontend assets.

docs.title: Runtime Behavior
docs.text:
Once integrated mode is enabled and the API is running:

  • Web UI is served at /
  • API is served at /api
  • API docs are served at /api/docs

docs.title: Example endpoints
docs.text:
text http://localhost:8080/ http://localhost:8080/api/projects http://localhost:8080/api/docs

Path consistency matters

The frontend must know the correct API base path. If the frontend expects /api but the API is mounted elsewhere, requests will fail.

docs.title: Required .env Variables
docs.text:
A typical integrated-mode configuration includes:

dotenv SERVE_WEB=1 API_MOUNT_PATH=/api WEB_DIST_PATH=/path/to/dist WEB_API_BASE_URL=/api

docs.title: Variable roles
docs.text:
• SERVE_WEB=1 enables frontend serving from the API
  • API_MOUNT_PATH=/api defines where the API is mounted
  • WEB_DIST_PATH points to the compiled frontend dist/ directory (or deployed assets path)
  • WEB_API_BASE_URL=/api tells the frontend where to call the API

Keep these aligned

In most setups, API_MOUNT_PATH and WEB_API_BASE_URL should be the same value (usually /api).

docs.title: Typical Deployment Flow
docs.text:
One-shot provisioning (recommended)Manual integrated mode

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe" \ --web-dist "$HOME/scipionweb/ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip"

This is the easiest path for a fresh local/server deployment.

  1. Extract the Web bundle
  2. Set integrated-mode variables in SCIPION_HOME/.env
  3. Ensure WEB_DIST_PATH points to the deployed dist/
  4. Restart the API service

docs.title: When to Use Integrated Mode
docs.text:
Integrated mode is a good fit for:

  • Simple deployments
  • Single-host setups
  • Local installations
  • Internal research lab servers
  • Environments where minimizing operational complexity is a priority

Great default for many labs

If you do not need CDN hosting, independent frontend deployments, or complex edge routing, integrated mode is often the most practical choice.

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Single service endpoint for users
  • No CORS issues in the common case (same origin)
  • Simpler networking
  • Easier installation and upgrades
  • Fewer moving parts for local/on-prem deployments

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Cannot scale frontend independently from the API
  • Static frontend assets are served by the API process
  • Less flexible for CDN-based frontend delivery
  • Frontend updates are often tied to API deployment workflow

Not a blocker for most small deployments

These limitations are usually acceptable for local, internal, or moderate-scale environments.

docs.title: Verification Checklist
docs.text: After enabling integrated mode:

docs.title: 1. Check API health
docs.text:
bash curl http://localhost:8080/health

Expected response:

json {"status":"ok"}

docs.title: 2. Open the Web UI
docs.text:
Open:

text http://localhost:8080/

docs.title: 3. Open API docs
docs.text:
Open:

text http://localhost:8080/api/docs

docs.title: 4. Inspect browser requests (optional)
docs.text: Confirm frontend API calls go to /api/... and not to an incorrect host/path.

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Frontend loads but API calls fail

Usually caused by a mismatch between API_MOUNT_PATH and WEB_API_BASE_URL, or a reverse proxy that rewrites paths incorrectly.

WEB_DIST_PATH points to old assets

After upgrades, verify that WEB_DIST_PATH references the current deployed frontend assets.

Got /docs instead of /api/docs

In integrated mode, docs are commonly served under the API mount path (for example /api/docs), not at /docs.

Blank page or missing static assets

Check that the frontend dist/ contents were deployed correctly and that the API can read the files.

docs.title: Recommended Practices
docs.text:
• Keep API and Web versions aligned when possible
  • Use integrated mode for simpler single-host deployments
  • Re-check .env paths after upgrades
  • Put a reverse proxy (nginx) in front for HTTPS/public access in production
  • Back up SCIPION_HOME/.env before major changes
← Previous: Environment Variables (.env) Next: Separate Deployment (API and Web on Different Hosts) →

docs.title: SCIPION_HOME and Runtime Layout
docs.text:
SCIPION_HOME is the runtime workspace directory used by ScipionAPI.

It stores runtime configuration, logs, project data, and (in integrated mode) deployed Web assets. This directory is part of your operational state and must not be committed to version control.

Why SCIPION_HOME exists

SCIPION_HOME keeps runtime data separate from the API source/bundle directory, which makes upgrades safer and helps preserve data across version changes.

docs.title: Default Location
docs.text:
If SCIPION_HOME is not set explicitly, ScipionAPI typically uses:

text SCIPION_HOME=<repoRoot>/scipion_home

You can override it by exporting the variable before starting the application:

bash export SCIPION_HOME=/opt/scipionweb/runtime

Production recommendation

In production, use a dedicated path outside the API bundle directory (for example /opt/scipionweb/runtime) so upgrades do not affect runtime data.

docs.title: Typical Directory Structure
docs.text:
Example layout:

text scipion_home/ ├── .env ├── logs/ │ ├── app.log │ └── celery.log ├── projects/ │ └── <project folders> ├── config/ └── web/ └── dist/

Mode-dependent directories

The web/ directory is typically present only in integrated mode (when the API serves the Web UI).

docs.title: What Each Path Is Used For
.env
docs.text:
Primary runtime configuration file.

It typically contains:

  • Database connection settings
  • Security-related values (for example SECRET_KEY)
  • Runtime paths
  • Deployment mode flags
  • API and broker configuration

Sensitive file

The .env file may contain credentials and secrets. Protect file permissions and never commit it to version control.

docs.title: logs/
docs.text:
Stores runtime logs, commonly including:

  • API logs
  • Celery worker logs

Typical uses:

  • Debugging errors
  • Operational monitoring
  • Post-incident inspection

Log rotation

In production, plan a log rotation strategy (system-managed logs, logrotate, or journald-based logging depending on your deployment approach).

docs.title: projects/
docs.text:
Stores project runtime data, such as:

  • Scipion projects
  • Generated previews
  • Runtime artifacts

This directory can grow significantly over time depending on project size and usage patterns.

Disk usage growth

Monitor available disk space and define a backup/retention strategy, especially for production or shared environments.

docs.title: config/
docs.text:
Optional location for Scipion-related runtime configuration files and generated configuration state, depending on your deployment and tooling.

Contents may vary

The exact contents of config/ may differ across versions or deployment workflows.

docs.title: web/
docs.text:
Present when using integrated mode.

Typical contents:

text web/dist/ index.html assets/ config.js

This directory stores the deployed frontend assets served by the API.

Web bundle upgrades

When upgrading the integrated Web UI, the deployed contents under web/ may be replaced by a new --web-dist deployment.

docs.title: Permissions and Ownership
docs.text:
Ensure that the service user (or the user running ScipionAPI) has the correct access to SCIPION_HOME.

Minimum recommendations:

  • Read/write access to SCIPION_HOME
  • Write access to logs/, projects/, and deployed runtime paths
  • Read access to .env
  • Enough free disk space for project growth and logs

Permission mismatches

Avoid mixing root-owned files with services running as a non-root user. This is a common cause of startup failures and runtime write errors.

docs.title: Backup Strategy
docs.text:
A practical backup strategy should include:

  • PostgreSQL database backup
  • Entire scipion_home/projects/ directory
  • SCIPION_HOME/.env
  • Any deployment-specific runtime configuration you rely on

Upgrade safety

Before upgrading ScipionAPI/ScipionWeb, back up both the database and key SCIPION_HOME contents so you can recover configuration and project data if needed.

docs.title: Production Recommendations
docs.text:
For production deployments, prefer a dedicated runtime path such as:

text /opt/scipionweb/runtime

instead of storing runtime data inside the API source or extracted bundle directory.

Recommended practices:

  • Keep SCIPION_HOME outside version-controlled directories
  • Protect .env permissions
  • Monitor disk usage in projects/ and logs/
  • Back up runtime data regularly
  • Reuse the same SCIPION_HOME across API bundle upgrades (when appropriate)

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
Using a temporary directory

Do not place SCIPION_HOME under /tmp or other ephemeral locations. You may lose logs, configuration, and project data.

Forgetting to export SCIPION_HOME after upgrade

If you move to a new API bundle directory and forget to export the existing SCIPION_HOME, the application may create a new empty runtime workspace and appear misconfigured.

Committing runtime files

Avoid committing scipion_home/ or .env files into Git repositories.

docs.title: Quick Verification
docs.text:
Run these checks to confirm the runtime workspace is correctly configured:

bash echo "$SCIPION_HOME" ls -al "$SCIPION_HOME" test -f "$SCIPION_HOME/.env" && echo ".env found" test -d "$SCIPION_HOME/projects" && echo "projects/ found" test -d "$SCIPION_HOME/logs" && echo "logs/ found"

If a path is missing

Some directories may be created lazily depending on how the application is used. The most critical item to verify early is SCIPION_HOME/.env.

← Previous: Configuration Overview Next: Environment Variables (.env) →

docs.title: Separate Deployment (API and Web on Different Hosts)
docs.text:
In a separate deployment, the API backend and the Web frontend are deployed independently.

Typical architecture:

  • API runs on one server (or service)
  • Web frontend runs on another host, static server, or CDN

Production-friendly pattern

This deployment model is common in production environments because it enables independent scaling, frontend hosting optimization, and clearer separation of responsibilities.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
A simplified request flow looks like this:

text User Browser → Web Server / CDN → REST API → PostgreSQL / Redis

In practice:

  • The browser loads the frontend from a web host (or CDN)
  • The frontend sends API requests to the ScipionAPI host
  • The API talks to PostgreSQL and Redis

docs.title: When to Use Separate Deployment
docs.text:
This mode is recommended for:

  • Production environments
  • Cloud deployments
  • Enterprise setups
  • Load-balanced infrastructure
  • CDN/static hosting for the frontend
  • Teams that deploy frontend and backend independently

Best for scale and flexibility

If you need independent release cycles or web performance optimization, separate deployment is usually the better choice.

docs.title: Frontend Configuration
docs.text: The frontend must know the public base URL of the API.

docs.title: Frontend .env (example)
docs.text: dotenv VITE_API_URL="https://api.yourdomain.com"

docs.title: Frontend config usage example
docs.text:
ts export const BASE_URL = import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL;

After changing frontend environment variables, rebuild (or redeploy) the frontend.

Rebuild required

For most Vite-based frontends, environment variables are injected at build time. Changing VITE_API_URL usually requires a rebuild.

docs.title: API Configuration
docs.text: In separate deployment mode, the API should not serve the frontend.

docs.title: Disable integrated mode
docs.text: dotenv SERVE_WEB=0

docs.title: CORS configuration
docs.text:
The API must allow the frontend origin(s). Example backend configuration:

python allow_origins=["http://web-host:5173"]

Production example:

python allow_origins=["https://yourdomain.com"]

CORS is required

If CORS is not configured correctly, browser requests may fail even when the API is healthy and reachable.

docs.title: API server
docs.text: text https://api.yourdomain.com

docs.title: Web server (frontend)
docs.text: text https://yourdomain.com

docs.title: Example API request from the frontend
docs.text:
text https://api.yourdomain.com/api/projects

Consistent public URLs

Keep the frontend's configured API URL aligned with the actual public API endpoint exposed through your reverse proxy / load balancer.

docs.title: TLS / HTTPS
docs.text:
Use HTTPS on both sides in production:

  • Frontend host (web app)
  • API host (backend)

docs.title: Firewalls / security groups
docs.text:
Allow inbound access only to what is needed:

  • Public web ports (80 / 443) on frontend host
  • Public or controlled access to API host (usually 443 behind a reverse proxy)
  • Internal database/Redis access restricted to trusted hosts/services

docs.title: Reverse proxy alignment
docs.text:
If the API is mounted under /api, ensure your reverse proxy configuration preserves the expected path.

Path rewrite mistakes

Misconfigured reverse proxies can strip or duplicate /api, causing 404s even though the backend is running.

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Independent scaling of frontend and backend
  • CDN support and static hosting optimization
  • Better frontend performance at scale
  • Clear separation of concerns
  • Independent deployment pipelines

docs.title: Considerations and Tradeoffs
docs.text:
• CORS configuration is required
  • SSL/TLS must be configured correctly
  • More moving parts than integrated mode
  • DNS, proxy, and firewall configuration become part of deployment operations

Operational complexity vs flexibility

Separate deployment adds infrastructure complexity, but it pays off in flexibility and scalability for larger environments.

docs.title: Frontend
docs.text:
• [ ] VITE_API_URL points to the correct public API URL
  • [ ] Frontend rebuilt/redeployed after env changes
  • [ ] HTTPS enabled on the frontend host

docs.title: API
docs.text:
• [ ] SERVE_WEB=0
  • [ ] CORS allows the frontend origin(s)
  • [ ] API mounted and proxied correctly (for example /api)
  • [ ] HTTPS enabled on the public API endpoint

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text:
• [ ] DNS resolves frontend and API domains correctly
  • [ ] Firewall/security groups allow required traffic only
  • [ ] Reverse proxy forwards API paths as expected

docs.title: 1. Check API health directly
docs.text:
bash curl https://api.yourdomain.com/health

(adjust the URL/path to your actual public API endpoint)

docs.title: 2. Test a browser session
docs.text:
• Open the frontend URL
  • Log in (if applicable)
  • Open browser developer tools
  • Confirm API requests go to the expected API domain/path

docs.title: 3. Validate CORS behavior
docs.text: Look for browser console/network errors such as blocked cross-origin requests.

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
CORS error in browser

The frontend origin is missing from the backend CORS allow list (or the scheme/port does not match exactly).

Frontend still calls old API host

VITE_API_URL was changed but the frontend was not rebuilt/redeployed.

API reachable from curl but not from browser

This usually indicates a CORS issue, mixed-content issue (HTTPS page calling HTTP API), or an incorrect frontend API base URL.

404 on /api/... behind reverse proxy

Check proxy path forwarding and backend mount path alignment.

docs.title: Integrated vs Separate Deployment (Quick Comparison)
docs.text:
Integrated ModeSeparate Deployment
  • Simpler setup
  • Single host / single endpoint
  • No CORS in common deployments
  • Easier for local/internal use
  • More flexible and scalable
  • Frontend and API deploy independently
  • CORS and proxy configuration required
  • Better fit for cloud/production platforms
← Previous: API + Web Integrated Mode

docs.title: Common Errors and Fixes
docs.text:
This page collects frequent development-time problems and the fastest checks to resolve them.

Use this page for local dev issues

If your problem is more related to deployed services, production startup, or runtime operations, also check Backend Troubleshooting.

docs.title: Quick Triage Checklist
docs.text:
Before going deep, verify the basics:

bash conda activate scipion4Web ./scripts/scipionapi status curl http://localhost:8080/health redis-cli ping

Then confirm:

  • you are using the expected Conda environment
  • SCIPION_HOME points to the right runtime workspace
  • .env exists and contains the expected values
  • PostgreSQL and Redis are reachable
  • API and Celery are reading the same runtime configuration

docs.title: Typical cause
docs.text: You are not running inside the expected Conda environment, or the worker was started outside the environment prepared by the wrapper script.

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
bash conda activate scipion4Web python -c "import pyworkflow; print(pyworkflow.__file__)"

If you are using the wrapper script, rerun the command from the ScipionAPI bundle directory:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi restart

docs.title: Symptom
docs.text: text KeyError: DATABASE_URL

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
• Ensure SCIPION_HOME/.env exists
  • Ensure SCIPION_HOME is exported correctly
  • Restart the API after changing environment values

Useful check:

bash echo "$SCIPION_HOME" test -f "$SCIPION_HOME/.env" && echo ".env found"

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• missing table errors
  • duplicate revision errors
  • multiple heads
  • Can't locate revision identified by ...

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
• inspect migration state with alembic heads and alembic current
  • verify you are targeting the correct database
  • avoid editing migrations that were already applied
  • test on a fresh empty database when state is unclear

For real deployments, create a database backup before stamping or repairing migration state.

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
bash sudo systemctl start redis-server sudo systemctl status redis-server redis-cli ping

Expected output:

text PONG

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• the worker is running
  • Redis is reachable
  • the broker URL matches between API and worker
  • worker logs show task receipt/execution
  • SCIPION_HOME is available to the worker process

docs.title: Useful commands
docs.text:
bash ./scripts/scipionapi status ./scripts/scipionapi logs redis-cli ping

If tasks stay in PENDING, confirm both the API and the worker are using the same runtime configuration.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• requests work with curl but fail in the browser
  • preflight or origin errors appear in DevTools

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
• add the exact frontend origin to the backend CORS configuration
  • verify scheme, host, and port match the real frontend URL
  • check that reverse proxies are not rewriting origins unexpectedly

For local development, the browser origin is usually similar to:

text http://localhost:5173

The API may be running at:

text http://localhost:8080

Both values must match the configured development setup.

docs.title: 7. JWT invalid signature or invalid token
Typical causes
docs.text:
• SECRET_KEY mismatch
  • expired token
  • stale token stored in the browser

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
• ensure the API is using the expected SECRET_KEY
  • clear stored tokens or log in again
  • restart the backend if configuration changed recently

A private browser window is a quick way to test whether stale local state is involved.

docs.title: Symptom
docs.text: text Address already in use

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
Identify the process first:

bash lsof -i :8080

Then stop it only if it is safe to do so:

bash kill <pid>

Use forceful termination only as a last resort:

bash kill -9 <pid>

Or change the configured API port if another service is meant to keep using that port.

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
bash chmod -R u+rwX "$SCIPION_HOME"

Also confirm that the same user owns the runtime files and starts the services:

bash ls -ld "$SCIPION_HOME" ls -ld "$SCIPION_HOME/logs"

docs.title: 10. When to escalate to backend troubleshooting
docs.text:
Go to Backend Troubleshooting when the issue involves:

  • deployed services that do not start correctly
  • database connectivity in shared or production environments
  • Redis or Celery runtime mismatches
  • authentication problems that reproduce outside local development

For recurring operational problems, also check Known Issues and Workarounds.

docs.title: Suggested Debugging Order
docs.text:
1. Check the active Conda environment
  2. Validate SCIPION_HOME and .env
  3. Test PostgreSQL and Redis reachability
  4. Review API and Celery logs
  5. Restart services and retry the failing workflow

docs.title: Local Development Workflow
docs.text:
This guide describes the recommended workflow for developing ScipionAPI locally.

It assumes:

  • Linux environment
  • Conda installed
  • PostgreSQL and Redis running locally

docs.title: 1. Clone Repository
docs.text: bash git clone <repository-url> cd ScipionAPI

docs.title: 2. Bootstrap Environment
docs.text:
bash ./scripts/scipionapi bootstrap

Activate environment:

bash conda activate scipion4Web

docs.title: 3. Configure Runtime Workspace
docs.text:
Create a development runtime directory:

bash export SCIPION_HOME=$(pwd)/scipion_home mkdir -p "$SCIPION_HOME"

Run install:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi install \ --user admin \ --email admin@local \ --pass changeMe

docs.title: 4. Start Development Server
docs.text:
Instead of using start, run uvicorn manually:

bash uvicorn app.backend.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080

This enables auto-reload on code changes.

docs.title: 5. Start Celery Worker
docs.text:
In another terminal:

bash celery -A app.workers.task_queue worker --loglevel=info

docs.title: 6. Verify
docs.text:
Open:

http://localhost:8080/docs

docs.title: Recommended Workflow
docs.text:
During development:

  • Use manual uvicorn with --reload
  • Do not use detached start
  • Keep logs visible in terminal
  • Use separate database for development

docs.title: Frontend Local Development
docs.text:
If developing frontend:

npm run dev

Ensure:

VITE_API_URL="http://localhost:8080"

docs.title: Suggested Branch Strategy
docs.text:
• main → stable
  • devel → active development
  • feature/* → feature branches

docs.title: Debugging with PyCharm
docs.text: This guide explains how to configure PyCharm for backend debugging.

docs.title: 1. Open Project
docs.text: Open the ScipionAPI directory in PyCharm.

docs.title: 2. Configure Python Interpreter
docs.text:
Go to:

Settings → Project → Python Interpreter

Select:

Conda environment → scipion4Web

If not detected, manually point to:

~/miniconda3/envs/scipion4Web/bin/python

docs.title: 3. Create Run Configuration
docs.text:
Create a new Python configuration:

  • Script path: leave empty
  • Module name:

uvicorn

Parameters:

app.backend.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080

Working directory:

<project-root>

Environment variables:

SCIPION_HOME=/absolute/path/to/scipion_home

docs.title: 4. Enable Debugging
docs.text:
Set breakpoints inside:

  • routers
  • services
  • models
  • auth dependencies

Run in Debug mode.

docs.title: 5. Debug Celery Tasks
docs.text:
For Celery debugging:

Instead of running separate worker, temporarily call task synchronously in code.

Or configure Celery to run in eager mode (development only).

docs.title: Common Debug Tips
docs.text:
• Ensure correct Conda interpreter selected
  • Ensure SCIPION_HOME is set
  • Verify .env is loaded
  • Use PyCharm database tool to inspect PostgreSQL

docs.title: Recommended PyCharm Settings
docs.text:
• Enable "Reload changed modules"
  • Enable "Show console"
  • Disable aggressive auto-import if interfering

docs.title: Running Uvicorn Manually
docs.text: For development, it is recommended to run Uvicorn manually instead of using the CLI start command.

docs.title: Basic Command
docs.text: bash uvicorn app.backend.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080

docs.title: Why Use --reload?
docs.text:
• Automatically reloads on file changes
  • Faster development cycle
  • No need to restart manually

docs.title: Required Environment
docs.text:
Before running:

bash conda activate scipion4Web export SCIPION_HOME=$(pwd)/scipion_home

Ensure .env exists inside SCIPION_HOME.

docs.title: Running on Different Port
docs.text: bash uvicorn app.backend.main:app --reload --port 9000

docs.title: Production Warning
docs.text:
Do NOT use:

--reload

In production.

Production should use:

  • systemd
  • gunicorn + uvicorn workers
  • reverse proxy

docs.title: Example Production Command
docs.text: uvicorn app.backend.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
If import errors appear:

  • Verify correct Conda environment
  • Verify PYTHONPATH includes project root
  • Verify SCIPION_HOME is set
