docs.title: Frontend Overview
docs.text:
ScipionWeb is the React-based frontend that provides the graphical user interface for ScipionAPI.

It is built with a modern web stack and distributed as a compiled static bundle that can be deployed in multiple modes.

Scope of this section

This section covers the frontend application model, build pipeline, runtime API URL configuration, and deployment options. Backend internals are documented in the Backend section.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
The frontend is responsible for:

  • User authentication (login/logout)
  • Project browsing
  • Protocol management
  • Output visualization
  • Plugin interaction
  • UI rendering and routing

Backend boundary

Business logic, persistence, and task execution are handled by the backend API. The frontend acts as a client application consuming REST endpoints.

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text:
• UI Framework React + TypeScript

Component-based UI with typed application code.

  • Build Tooling Vite

Fast local development server and optimized production builds.

  • API Communication REST over HTTP

The frontend communicates with ScipionAPI using a configurable base URL.

  • Distribution Model Static dist/ bundle

Deployable via API-integrated mode, web servers, or CDN hosting.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
High-level request flow:

text Browser → React App → REST API → Backend

The frontend communicates with ScipionAPI through HTTP requests only.

docs.title: Build and Distribution Model
docs.text:
ScipionWeb is typically:

  • Developed in a separate repository
  • Built using Vite
  • Packaged as a static dist/ bundle
  • Served either by:
  • ScipionAPI (integrated mode)
  • A separate web server (separate deployment)
  • CDN/static hosting

docs.title: Runtime API Configuration
docs.text:
The frontend API base URL is controlled by:

dotenv VITE_API_URL

Build-time + runtime behavior

The frontend can use VITE_API_URL at build time and may also consume injected runtime configuration in integrated mode.

See: Runtime API URL Configuration

docs.title: Deployment Modes
docs.text:
ScipionWeb supports three main deployment strategies:

  1. Integrated mode (API serves frontend)
  2. Separate deployment (frontend and API on different hosts)
  3. CDN/static hosting (frontend on CDN, API separate)

See: Deployment Options

docs.title: Version Compatibility
docs.text:
The compiled frontend bundle should match a compatible ScipionAPI version.

Recommended practice:

  • Upgrade API and Web together
  • Keep versioned frontend dist bundles
  • Validate API compatibility before production rollout

Common upgrade pitfall

A frontend bundle can load successfully while still failing at runtime if the API version is incompatible (routes, payloads, auth behavior, or config expectations).

docs.title: Recommended Reading Order
docs.text:
1. Build and Dist Output
  2. Runtime API URL Configuration
  3. Deployment Options

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: Backend Overview         Next: Build and Dist Output →

docs.title: Build and Dist Output
docs.text:
ScipionWeb frontend builds are generated with Vite.

This page covers:

  • Local development mode
  • Production builds
  • dist/ output layout
  • Packaging for distribution
  • Integration with ScipionAPI provisioning

docs.title: Development Mode
docs.text:
From inside the frontend repository:

bash npm install npm run dev

This starts a local development server (typically):

text http://localhost:5173

Frontend-backend pairing

During local development, ensure the backend API is running and reachable from the browser.

docs.title: Development API URL
docs.text:
The frontend API URL should be defined in:

text .env

Example:

dotenv VITE_API_URL="http://localhost:8080"

Dev env changes require restart

If you change .env, restart the Vite dev server to ensure the new values are applied.

docs.title: Production Build
docs.text:
Generate the production bundle:

bash npm run build

This creates a static output directory:

text dist/ ├── index.html ├── assets/ └── ...

docs.title: Dist Output Structure
docs.text:
Example production output:

text dist/ ├── index.html ├── assets/ │ ├── index-abc123.js │ ├── index-abc123.css │ └── ... └── favicon.ico

The dist/ folder is static and can be:

  • Served by nginx
  • Served by Apache
  • Hosted on a CDN/static platform
  • Mounted into ScipionAPI (integrated mode)

Hashed assets

File names inside assets/ are often content-hashed for cache busting. This is expected behavior in production builds.

docs.title: Packaging for Distribution
docs.text:
To distribute the compiled frontend bundle:

bash zip -r ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip dist/

This ZIP can be used with ScipionAPI provisioning:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision --web-dist ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip

Versioned artifacts

Keep versioned ZIP files (and optionally build metadata) to simplify rollback and compatibility tracking.

docs.title: Build Verification Checklist
docs.text:
After npm run build, verify:

  • dist/index.html exists
  • dist/assets/ contains JS/CSS bundles
  • The configured API URL strategy matches your deployment mode
  • The bundle version matches the target API version

docs.title: Important Notes
docs.text:
Do not edit dist/ manually

Treat dist/ as generated output. Make source changes in the frontend codebase and rebuild.

Rebuild after config changes

Changes to VITE_API_URL and other build-time env values require a new production build.

Check API compatibility

Ensure the frontend bundle and ScipionAPI version are compatible before production deployment.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: Frontend Overview         Next: Runtime API URL Configuration →

docs.title: Deployment Options
docs.text: ScipionWeb can be deployed in two main ways.

docs.title: Integrated mode
docs.text:
Integrated mode is the recommended option for most installations. In this mode, ScipionAPI serves both the API and the compiled Web UI from the same runtime.

Use this option for local labs, internal installations, and first-time deployments.

docs.title: Separate deployment
docs.text:
In separate deployment mode, the frontend and API are hosted independently.

Use this option only when your infrastructure requires independent frontend and backend hosting.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: Use integrated mode unless you have a specific reason to host the frontend separately.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Runtime API URL Configuration
  • Integrated Mode
  • Separate Deployment

docs.title: Runtime API URL Configuration
docs.text:
The frontend communicates with ScipionAPI using a base API URL.

This is primarily defined by:

dotenv VITE_API_URL

docs.title: Development
docs.text: dotenv VITE_API_URL="http://localhost:8080"

docs.title: Production (separate deployment example)
docs.text:
dotenv VITE_API_URL="https://api.yourdomain.com"

The variable is typically consumed as:

ts export const BASE_URL = import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL;

Vite behavior

Values from VITE_* variables are injected into the frontend build and are not automatically updated after build time.

docs.title: Integrated Mode (API + Web)
docs.text:
In integrated mode:

  • Web is served at /
  • API is mounted at /api

No external API host is required for the frontend.

The installer can inject runtime configuration through:

text window.__SCIPION_WEB_CONFIG__

Example payload:

json { "apiBaseUrl": "/api" }

The frontend can prefer this runtime value when present, allowing a single build artifact to adapt to integrated deployments.

Why runtime injection helps

Runtime config avoids rebuilding the frontend just to change the API mount path in integrated scenarios.

docs.title: Separate Deployment
docs.text:
If frontend and API run on different hosts, define the API URL in the frontend .env before building:

dotenv VITE_API_URL="https://api.yourdomain.com"

Then rebuild the frontend:

bash npm run build

docs.title: Configuration Matrix
docs.text: Deployment Mode Frontend Host API Host Typical API Base Integrated Same as API Same as frontend /api Separate https://yourdomain.com https://api.yourdomain.com https://api.yourdomain.com CDN CDN/static host Separate API host https://api.yourdomain.com

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
Changing .env without rebuilding

In production builds, VITE_API_URL is compiled into the bundle. Rebuild after changes.

HTTP/HTTPS mismatch

Mixed protocol usage (frontend on HTTPS, API on HTTP) can break requests in browsers.

CORS not configured on backend

Separate deployments require backend CORS configuration for the frontend origin.

Using localhost in production builds

Double-check build artifacts before release to avoid accidentally shipping localhost API URLs.

docs.title: Production Recommendations
docs.text:
• Use HTTPS for frontend and API
  • Use stable domain naming (app. / api. pattern, or integrated /api)
  • Document your API URL strategy per environment (dev/staging/prod)
  • Validate API reachability from the browser before rollout

docs.title: Quick Validation
docs.text:
After deployment, confirm in the browser:

  • Login requests go to the expected API origin/path
  • No CORS errors appear in DevTools
  • No mixed-content warnings appear
  • Network requests return expected responses

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: ← Previous: Build and Dist Output         Next: Deployment Options →

docs.title: Installation Overview
docs.text:
Use this section when you want to install ScipionWeb for users or upgrade an existing installation.

For most installations, the recommended path is Integrated Mode (API + Web). In that mode, ScipionAPI serves the compiled ScipionWeb interface and the API from the same runtime, so users can open the web application directly in the browser.

Recommended path for most installations

If you are installing for the first time on a local Linux machine, follow this sequence:

  1. Prerequisites
  2. Download and Extract Bundles
  3. Recommended Installation

docs.title: Recommended installation
docs.text:
Use Prerequisites → Download Bundles → Recommended Installation when you want the fastest path to a working ScipionWeb instance with both API and Web UI.

This is the normal path for user-facing installations.

docs.title: Manual installation
docs.text:
Use Manual Installation only when you need full control over each layer, for example:

  • remote PostgreSQL setup
  • debugging installation problems
  • custom deployment requirements
  • development environments

docs.title: Upgrade or reinstall
docs.text: Use Upgrade / Reinstall Notes when an existing installation already exists and you need to preserve runtime data, database state, and configuration.

docs.title: What this section covers
docs.text:
This installation section includes:

  • system prerequisites such as Conda, PostgreSQL, Redis, ports, and permissions
  • official bundle download and extraction
  • recommended integrated installation using provision and --web-dist
  • manual installation step by step for advanced setups
  • upgrade workflow and rollback guidance

docs.title: 1. Prerequisites
docs.text:
Prepare the machine and verify required services before installation.

➡️ Open Prerequisites

docs.title: 2. Download and Extract Bundles
docs.text:
Download the official ScipionAPI and ScipionWeb bundles and prepare the installation directory layout.

➡️ Open Download and Extract Bundles

docs.title: 3. Recommended Installation
docs.text:
Run a complete integrated installation using a single command.

➡️ Open Recommended Installation

docs.title: 4. Manual Installation
docs.text:
Install ScipionAPI step by step for maximum control.

➡️ Open Manual Installation

docs.title: 5. Upgrade / Reinstall Notes
docs.text:
Upgrade an existing installation safely.

➡️ Open Upgrade / Reinstall Notes

docs.title: Common installation split
docs.text:
A useful way to think about installation is:

  1. machine readiness → prerequisites
  2. artifacts → bundles
  3. runtime setup → recommended installation or manual installation
  4. service verification → browser, health endpoint, logs, and project loading

This mental split makes debugging much easier when something goes wrong.

docs.title: Quick notes
docs.text:
Integrated mode is the normal user-facing mode

If you want users to open ScipionWeb in the browser, download the ScipionWeb compiled bundle and use the --web-dist option during provision.

Backup before upgrade

Always create a PostgreSQL backup before running an upgrade, especially in production or shared environments.

Consistency matters

Keep ScipionAPI and ScipionWeb versions aligned whenever possible to reduce compatibility issues.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: Next: Prerequisites →

docs.title: Production Deployment with systemd
docs.text:
This guide explains how to run ScipionAPI and the Celery worker as systemd services.

Using systemd is recommended for:

  • Production environments
  • Automatic restart on failure
  • Startup on boot
  • Centralized logging with journalctl
  • Stable long-running services

Scope

This page covers a direct systemd deployment (API + Celery worker). If you expose the service to users, a reverse proxy such as nginx is strongly recommended.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A typical production deployment with systemd includes:

  1. Stop CLI-managed services (if running)
  2. Identify installation paths
  3. Create a systemd service for the API
  4. Create a systemd service for the Celery worker
  5. Reload systemd
  6. Enable services on boot
  7. Start services
  8. Verify status and logs

Prerequisite

Make sure the installation is already working (for example via provision) before switching to systemd.

docs.title: 1. Stop CLI-Managed Services
docs.text:
If you previously started services with the ScipionAPI CLI, stop them first to avoid port conflicts and duplicate workers.

bash ./scripts/scipionapi stop

Avoid mixed process management

Do not run the same API/worker both through the CLI and systemd at the same time.

docs.title: 2. Locate Installation Paths
docs.text: Identify the paths used by your deployment.

docs.title: Example API installation directory
docs.text: text /opt/scipionweb/ScipionAPI-1.0.0

docs.title: Example SCIPION_HOME
docs.text: text /opt/scipionweb/scipion_home

docs.title: Example Conda environment
docs.text:
text /home/youruser/miniconda3/envs/scipion4Web

Use absolute paths only

Always use absolute paths in systemd service files (WorkingDirectory, ExecStart, SCIPION_HOME, etc.).

docs.title: 3. Create the systemd Service for the API
docs.text:
Create the service file:

text /etc/systemd/system/scipionapi.service

docs.title: Example scipionapi.service
docs.text:
```ini [Unit] Description=ScipionAPI Service After=network.target postgresql.service redis-server.service Wants=postgresql.service redis-server.service

[Service] Type=simple User=youruser WorkingDirectory=/opt/scipionweb/ScipionAPI-1.0.0 Environment=SCIPION_HOME=/opt/scipionweb/scipion_home ExecStart=/home/youruser/miniconda3/envs/scipion4Web/bin/uvicorn app.backend.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080 Restart=always RestartSec=5

[Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target ```

Service dependencies

After= controls startup order. Wants= asks systemd to also start PostgreSQL and Redis when starting the API service.

docs.title: 4. Create the systemd Service for the Celery Worker
docs.text:
Create the service file:

text /etc/systemd/system/scipion-celery.service

docs.title: Example scipion-celery.service
docs.text:
```ini [Unit] Description=ScipionAPI Celery Worker After=network.target redis-server.service Wants=redis-server.service

[Service] Type=simple User=youruser WorkingDirectory=/opt/scipionweb/ScipionAPI-1.0.0 Environment=SCIPION_HOME=/opt/scipionweb/scipion_home ExecStart=/home/youruser/miniconda3/envs/scipion4Web/bin/celery -A app.workers.task_queue worker --loglevel=info Restart=always RestartSec=5

[Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target ```

Celery and API separation

Keeping the API and Celery worker in separate services makes restarts, monitoring, and troubleshooting much easier.

docs.title: 5. Reload systemd
docs.text:
Reload the systemd daemon after creating or modifying service files:

bash sudo systemctl daemon-reload

docs.title: 6. Enable Services on Boot
docs.text:
Enable both services so they start automatically after reboot:

bash sudo systemctl enable scipionapi sudo systemctl enable scipion-celery

docs.title: 7. Start Services
docs.text:
Start both services:

bash sudo systemctl start scipionapi sudo systemctl start scipion-celery

Start order

Start order between API and Celery is usually flexible, but Redis must be available before the worker can process tasks.

docs.title: 8. Check Status
docs.text:
Verify both services are active:

bash sudo systemctl status scipionapi sudo systemctl status scipion-celery

For a quick summary:

bash systemctl is-active scipionapi systemctl is-active scipion-celery

Expected output:

text active active

docs.title: Logs
docs.text:
Follow logs in real time with journalctl:

bash journalctl -u scipionapi -f journalctl -u scipion-celery -f

Show recent logs only

Useful filters:

  • journalctl -u scipionapi -n 100 --no-pager
  • journalctl -u scipion-celery -n 100 --no-pager

docs.title: Optional: nginx Reverse Proxy
docs.text:
Using nginx in front of ScipionAPI is recommended for:

  • HTTPS termination
  • Exposing ports 80 / 443
  • Reverse proxy headers
  • Load balancing (advanced setups)

docs.title: Minimal nginx proxy example
docs.text:
nginx location / { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8080; proxy_set_header Host $host; proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr; proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for; proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme; }

Integrated mode paths

If you use integrated mode (frontend served by the API) and custom API mount paths (for example /api), verify nginx forwards requests without breaking those routes.

docs.title: Run with a dedicated service user
docs.text: Instead of a personal account, consider a dedicated Linux user (for example scipion) to run the services.

docs.title: Restrict file permissions
docs.text:
Protect:

  • SCIPION_HOME/.env
  • Logs (if they may contain sensitive paths or errors)
  • Project data directories

docs.title: Keep backups before upgrades
docs.text:
Before upgrading ScipionAPI/ScipionWeb, backup:

  • PostgreSQL database
  • SCIPION_HOME/.env
  • Critical project data (if applicable)

docs.title: Test service restart behavior
docs.text:
After deployment, verify service restarts:

bash sudo systemctl restart scipionapi sudo systemctl restart scipion-celery sudo systemctl status scipionapi scipion-celery

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Service fails to start

Check:

  • WorkingDirectory exists
  • ExecStart points to the correct Conda environment binaries
  • SCIPION_HOME is correct
  • PostgreSQL and Redis are running
  • The installation works when started manually

ModuleNotFoundError in systemd logs

Usually caused by an incorrect WorkingDirectory or the wrong Python/Celery binary path in ExecStart.

Port 8080 already in use

Another process is already bound to the port. Stop the conflicting process or change the API port.

Celery starts but tasks do not run

Verify Redis connectivity, broker URL configuration, and check worker logs for import/configuration errors.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Using systemd provides:

  • Automatic restart on failure
  • Startup on boot
  • Centralized logging with journalctl
  • Better operational stability for production deployments

A minimal production setup typically includes:

  • scipionapi.service
  • scipion-celery.service
  • PostgreSQL
  • Redis
  • nginx (recommended for HTTPS/public exposure)
← Previous: Upgrade

docs.title: Download and Extract Bundles
docs.text:
This page explains how to download the official Scipion bundles and prepare the installation directory.

ScipionWeb is distributed as:

  • ScipionAPI bundle (server)
  • ScipionWeb bundle (compiled frontend, optional for integrated mode)

Integrated mode

The ScipionWeb bundle is optional if you plan to run the API without serving the frontend from the same installation.

docs.title: Official download location
docs.text:
All versioned bundles are published at:

https://scipion.cnb.csic.es/downloads/scipion/scipionWeb/

You normally need:

  • ScipionAPI-<version>.zip
  • ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip if you want integrated mode

Version placeholder

Replace <version> with the actual published version number.

The most important rule here is version alignment: try to keep API and Web bundles on the same release whenever possible.

docs.title: Recommended directory layout
docs.text:
A clean local layout looks like this:

text $HOME/scipionweb/ ├── ScipionAPI-<version>/ └── ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip

The Web ZIP can remain unextracted if you plan to pass it directly to provision using --web-dist.

docs.title: Download the bundles
docs.text:
Using wgetUsing curl

```bash mkdir -p "$HOME/scipionweb" cd "$HOME/scipionweb"

wget "https://scipion.cnb.csic.es/downloads/scipion/scipionWeb/ScipionAPI-.zip" wget "https://scipion.cnb.csic.es/downloads/scipion/scipionWeb/ScipionWeb--dist.zip" ```

```bash mkdir -p "$HOME/scipionweb" cd "$HOME/scipionweb"

curl -O "https://scipion.cnb.csic.es/downloads/scipion/scipionWeb/ScipionAPI-.zip" curl -O "https://scipion.cnb.csic.es/downloads/scipion/scipionWeb/ScipionWeb--dist.zip" ```

Optional Web bundle

If you do not need integrated mode, you may skip downloading ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip.

docs.title: Extract the API bundle
docs.text:
bash unzip ScipionAPI-<version>.zip cd ScipionAPI-<version>

At this point, the main thing to verify is that the API directory contains the wrapper script and the expected project structure.

docs.title: What to verify after extraction
docs.text:
Inside the extracted API directory, confirm that you can see expected files such as:

  • scripts/
  • scipionapi_cli/
  • app/
  • alembic/
  • pyproject.toml

A useful sanity check is making sure scripts/scipionapi exists before moving on.

docs.title: Web bundle usage
docs.text:
If you want integrated mode:

  • keep the Web ZIP file available
  • pass it directly to provision with --web-dist

Manual extraction of the Web bundle is optional and mostly useful for inspection.

If you want to inspect it manually, extract it with:

bash unzip ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip

The extracted contents should include:

text dist/ index.html assets/

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
Wrong or mismatched bundle versions

If API and Web versions drift too far apart, frontend and backend expectations may not align.

Extracting the wrong ZIP

Make sure you extracted the API bundle and not the Web bundle when looking for scripts/scipionapi.

Moving files before installation planning

Keep the layout simple until installation succeeds. Overcomplicated directory moves make debugging harder.

docs.title: Manual Installation
docs.text:
This guide describes how to install ScipionAPI manually, step by step.

Use this method if:

  • you prefer full control over the installation
  • you are using a remote PostgreSQL server
  • you do not want automatic database bootstrap
  • you are debugging or developing locally

Manual vs one-shot provisioning

If you want the fastest setup path, use provision. This page is intended for controlled, step-by-step installation.

docs.title: Mental model
docs.text:
Manual installation is the right path when you want to separate these stages clearly:

  1. Python environment
  2. database and runtime configuration
  3. migrations and admin creation
  4. service startup and verification

That separation is slower than provision, but much better for debugging and advanced deployments.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Manual installation typically consists of:

  1. creating a Conda environment
  2. installing Python dependencies
  3. configuring PostgreSQL manually
  4. running Alembic migrations
  5. creating the admin user
  6. starting API and Celery services

docs.title: 1. Create Conda Environment
docs.text:
Run the following commands from inside the extracted ScipionAPI directory:

bash conda create -n scipion4Web python=3.8 -y conda activate scipion4Web python -m pip install --upgrade pip

docs.title: 2. Install Dependencies
docs.text:
bash pip install -r requirements.txt pip install -e . scipionapi --help

Editable install

pip install -e . is recommended for local development because code changes are picked up without reinstalling the package.

docs.title: 3. Create PostgreSQL Database Manually
docs.text:
If you are not using automatic database bootstrap, create the PostgreSQL role and database manually.

Typical outline:

  • create role
  • create database
  • grant privileges
  • verify connectivity

The key point is to be sure the values you create here match what you will later place in .env.

docs.title: 4. Configure Environment (.env)
docs.text:
Create a runtime workspace such as:

bash mkdir -p scipion_home export SCIPION_HOME="$(pwd)/scipion_home"

Then create:

text scipion_home/.env

Populate it with a valid DATABASE_URL, SECRET_KEY, API host and port, broker URL, and runtime paths.

docs.title: 5. Run Alembic Migrations
docs.text:
Load the environment values and apply migrations:

bash set -a source scipion_home/.env set +a alembic upgrade head

At this point the database schema should be ready.

docs.title: 6. Create Admin User
docs.text:
Run:

bash scipionapi install \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe"

This step ensures the admin user exists and aligns runtime configuration with the installation state.

docs.title: 7. Start Services
docs.text:
Start the API and the Celery worker, then verify:

bash curl http://localhost:8080/health

If you are running manually, keep API and worker logs visible in separate terminals while testing.

docs.title: When this path is best
docs.text:
Manual installation is recommended for:

  • development environments
  • CI testing
  • remote PostgreSQL servers
  • debugging migration issues
  • custom deployment environments

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
Environment prepared, but .env still wrong

A healthy Python environment does not guarantee a healthy runtime configuration.

Database exists, but migration state is unclear

When in doubt, test the migration flow on a fresh empty database.

Services start, but worker side is forgotten

Always verify both the API and Celery sides of the runtime.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before installing ScipionAPI and optionally the compiled ScipionWeb bundle, prepare the target machine with the required system software, services, and permissions.

This guide assumes a Linux host. Ubuntu and Debian are the most straightforward environments for the documented commands.

Scope

This page covers system prerequisites only. It does not install ScipionAPI itself yet.

docs.title: What you need before installation
docs.text:
A typical ScipionWeb deployment requires:

  • Conda (Miniconda or Anaconda)
  • PostgreSQL
  • Redis
  • sudo privileges in common local-install scenarios
  • Internet access for Python package installation

The installation flow later uses these pieces to:

  • create the Python environment
  • configure the database
  • run migrations
  • start API and Celery services

docs.title: Recommended
docs.text:
• Ubuntu 22.04 / 24.04
  • Debian 12+

Other Linux distributions

Other Linux distributions may also work, but package names, service names, and service-management commands may differ.

docs.title: 1. Install Conda
docs.text: ScipionAPI uses Conda to manage its Python environment automatically.

docs.title: Check whether Conda is already installed
docs.text:
bash conda --version

If that prints a version number, continue to PostgreSQL.

docs.title: Install Miniconda (recommended)
docs.text:
If conda is not available, install Miniconda:

bash mkdir -p ~/Downloads cd ~/Downloads wget https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh bash Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh

Follow the installer prompts.

After installation, restart your terminal or run:

bash conda init bash exec bash

Then verify again:

bash conda --version

docs.title: If conda is still not found
docs.text:
Add Miniconda to your PATH manually:

bash export PATH="$HOME/miniconda3/bin:$PATH" conda --version

If you installed Anaconda instead, adjust the path accordingly:

bash export PATH="$HOME/anaconda3/bin:$PATH" conda --version

Make PATH changes persistent

If conda only works after manually editing PATH, add that change to your shell profile before continuing.

docs.title: 2. Install PostgreSQL
docs.text:
PostgreSQL stores application data such as:

  • users
  • projects
  • protocol metadata
  • sharing information
  • settings

In common local setups, the installer can create the database and role automatically, but only if PostgreSQL is installed, running, and reachable.

docs.title: Install PostgreSQL on Ubuntu or Debian
docs.text: bash sudo apt update sudo apt install -y postgresql postgresql-contrib

docs.title: Start and enable PostgreSQL
docs.text: bash sudo systemctl enable postgresql sudo systemctl start postgresql

docs.title: Verify PostgreSQL
docs.text: bash sudo systemctl status postgresql sudo -u postgres psql -c "SELECT version();"

docs.title: Why sudo matters
docs.text:
The installer can automatically create the PostgreSQL user and database in common local setups using commands such as:

bash sudo -u postgres psql ...

This usually requires:

  • a local PostgreSQL server
  • a valid sudo session
  • permission to run commands as the postgres system user

Manual database bootstrap

If you prefer not to use automatic database bootstrap, create the database and user manually before running the installer.

docs.title: 3. Install Redis
docs.text: Redis is used as the Celery broker and result backend.

docs.title: Install Redis on Ubuntu or Debian
docs.text: bash sudo apt update sudo apt install -y redis-server

docs.title: Start and enable Redis
docs.text: bash sudo systemctl enable redis-server sudo systemctl start redis-server

docs.title: Verify Redis
docs.text:
bash sudo systemctl status redis-server redis-cli ping

Expected output:

text PONG

docs.title: 4. Install recommended system utilities
docs.text:
Make sure the following utilities are available:

bash sudo apt update sudo apt install -y \ bash \ coreutils \ curl \ wget \ unzip \ tar \ git \ ca-certificates

Why these utilities?

They are commonly used during installation, troubleshooting, archive extraction, downloads, and repository operations.

docs.title: 5. Verify ports and permissions
docs.text:
By default, the services use:

  • API: 8080
  • PostgreSQL: 5432
  • Redis: 6379

Check whether those ports are already in use:

bash ss -ltnp | grep -E ':(8080|5432|6379)\b'

Also confirm that the installation user will have write access to:

  • the extracted ScipionAPI directory
  • the selected SCIPION_HOME location

Avoid permission mismatches

Do not mix root-owned files with a regular-user installation directory unless you intentionally manage ownership and permissions.

docs.title: 6. Pre-installation checklist
docs.text:
Before continuing, confirm that:

  • conda --version works
  • PostgreSQL is installed and running
  • Redis is installed and running
  • required utilities such as curl, wget, and unzip are available
  • you have sudo access if you want automatic local DB bootstrap
  • required ports are available
  • disk space is sufficient for bundles, runtime data, and logs

docs.title: Fast sanity check
docs.text:
Run these together:

bash conda --version sudo systemctl is-active postgresql sudo systemctl is-active redis-server redis-cli ping

You want to see:

  • a Conda version
  • active for PostgreSQL
  • active for Redis
  • PONG from Redis

docs.title: Conda not found
docs.text:
Usually a PATH or shell-initialization issue.

bash export PATH="$HOME/miniconda3/bin:$PATH" conda init bash exec bash

If the command works after exporting PATH, add the export line to your shell profile.

docs.title: PostgreSQL not starting
docs.text:
Inspect the service status and recent logs:

bash sudo systemctl status postgresql journalctl -u postgresql --no-pager -n 100

docs.title: Redis not responding
docs.text:
Inspect the service status and recent logs:

bash sudo systemctl status redis-server journalctl -u redis-server --no-pager -n 100

docs.title: sudo -u postgres fails
docs.text:
Try refreshing your sudo session:

bash sudo -v

If it still fails, manually create the PostgreSQL user and database before running the installer.

Common causes include:

  • your user is not in the sudoers group
  • sudo credentials expired
  • PostgreSQL service is not running
  • local PostgreSQL authentication rules were customized

docs.title: Provision (One-Shot Installation)
docs.text:
The provision command performs a complete installation in a single step.

For most user-facing installations, the recommended setup is Integrated Mode (API + Web). In this mode, ScipionAPI serves both the REST API and the compiled ScipionWeb interface from the same runtime.

It can:

  • bootstrap the Conda environment
  • install Python dependencies
  • create or update SCIPION_HOME
  • create PostgreSQL role and database in common local setups
  • run Alembic migrations
  • create or update the admin user
  • deploy and serve the Web bundle
  • start the API and Celery worker

What this page covers

This page documents the one-shot installation path using provision. If you need fine-grained control over each step, use the manual installation workflow instead.

docs.title: Recommended mode: Integrated Mode (API + Web)
docs.text:
Integrated mode is the recommended path for normal ScipionWeb users because it provides a complete working application:

  • Web UI served at /
  • API mounted at /api
  • API docs at /api/docs
  • API and Web assets deployed together

This avoids asking users to install the backend without the web interface they actually need to use the platform.

Run the command from inside the extracted ScipionAPI directory and pass the compiled Web bundle ZIP with --web-dist:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe" \ --web-dist "$HOME/scipionweb/ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip"

Credentials in shell history

Passing passwords directly on the command line may store them in your shell history. Use a temporary admin password for installation and change it afterwards if required.

docs.title: API-only mode
docs.text:
API-only mode is available, but it is mainly useful for developers, infrastructure testing, or deployments where the frontend is hosted separately.

Use it only if you intentionally do not want ScipionAPI to serve the compiled Web UI:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi provision \ --user "admin" \ --email "admin@example.com" \ --pass "changeMe"

In API-only mode:

  • the API is served directly by the backend
  • the compiled Web UI is not deployed by this command
  • users will need another frontend deployment to use ScipionWeb as an application

docs.title: When to choose provision
docs.text:
provision is the right default when:

  • you want the fastest supported setup path
  • you are installing on a local Linux machine
  • PostgreSQL and Redis are already available or easy to prepare
  • you want API and worker services started for you
  • you want the Web UI deployed together with the API

If you want to inspect every installation layer manually, use bootstrap + install instead.

docs.title: What provision does
docs.text:
A typical integrated-mode run performs these steps in sequence:

  1. prepares the Conda environment
  2. installs Python dependencies
  3. creates SCIPION_HOME
  4. generates .env
  5. prepares database state and migrations
  6. creates or updates the admin user
  7. deploys the Web bundle passed with --web-dist
  8. starts API and Celery as detached services

docs.title: What to verify after it finishes
docs.text:
Run these checks immediately:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi status ./scripts/scipionapi logs curl http://localhost:8080/health

For the recommended integrated mode, also verify that:

  • http://localhost:8080/ loads the Web UI
  • http://localhost:8080/api/docs opens correctly

docs.title: Re-running provision
docs.text:
You may safely re-run provision in most common cases:

  • it does not recreate the Conda environment if it already exists
  • it does not drop existing databases
  • it updates admin credentials if needed
  • it redeploys the Web bundle if --web-dist is provided again

That makes it useful after a failed first attempt once prerequisites are fixed.

docs.title: Common issues
docs.text:
Conda not found

Ensure conda --version works before running provision.

PostgreSQL authentication failed

Verify the configured database credentials match the actual PostgreSQL role and password.

Redis not running

Start Redis and retry.

Web UI does not load after provisioning

Confirm that --web-dist pointed to the correct ScipionWeb-<version>-dist.zip file and that the API was restarted after deployment.

Port already in use

If port 8080 is already in use, stop the conflicting service or update your configuration before provisioning.

docs.title: Upgrade Guide
docs.text:
This guide explains how to upgrade ScipionAPI and optionally ScipionWeb to a newer version.

Recommended approach

Treat upgrades as controlled operations: stop services, backup first, upgrade, then verify before resuming normal usage.

docs.title: Upgrade mental model
docs.text:
A safe upgrade usually means preserving three things correctly:

  1. the runtime workspace (SCIPION_HOME)
  2. the database state
  3. the version alignment between API and Web bundles

Most upgrade mistakes come from breaking one of those three.

docs.title: Typical upgrade flow
docs.text:
A common upgrade involves:

  1. stopping services
  2. backing up the database
  3. extracting the new API bundle
  4. reusing the existing SCIPION_HOME
  5. running provision from the new bundle
  6. optionally upgrading the Web bundle
  7. verifying health, logs, and basic UI behavior

docs.title: 1. Stop services
docs.text:
From the current installation directory:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi stop ./scripts/scipionapi status

Do not upgrade while services are still running.

docs.title: 2. Back up first
docs.text:
Before touching the new version, create a database backup.

This is the minimum safety step that makes rollback realistic.

If the deployment matters, also preserve the relevant runtime workspace state such as .env and project-related data.

docs.title: 3. Extract the new API bundle
docs.text:
Download the new bundle and extract it into a clean directory.

The important rule is: run the new version from the new bundle directory, but keep pointing to the existing runtime workspace.

docs.title: 4. Reuse existing SCIPION_HOME
docs.text:
Do not delete your existing runtime workspace.

That workspace usually contains:

  • .env
  • logs
  • projects
  • runtime configuration
  • deployed web assets in integrated setups

Before proceeding, verify that the reused .env still points to the correct database and services.

docs.title: 5. Run provision again
docs.text:
From the new API bundle directory, run provision again.

The practical goal here is:

  • refresh dependencies if needed
  • apply migrations
  • preserve data
  • bring runtime services back under the new version

docs.title: 6. Upgrade the Web bundle if needed
docs.text:
If you are using integrated mode, pass the new Web bundle with --web-dist during the new provision run.

Keep API and Web versions aligned whenever possible.

docs.title: 7. Verify before calling it done
docs.text:
Check:

  • status
  • /health
  • logs
  • login
  • project loading
  • one basic workflow action in the UI

The point is not only that processes run, but that the application behaves normally again.

docs.title: Rollback thinking
docs.text:
If the upgrade fails badly, rollback usually means:

  1. stop services
  2. restore the database backup
  3. go back to the previous API bundle
  4. restart from the previously known-good state

Rollback is much safer when the backup was created before any migration step.

docs.title: Common upgrade mistakes
docs.text:
Running the new code against the wrong runtime workspace

Always verify which SCIPION_HOME the new bundle is actually using.

Upgrading code without verified backup

If migrations change the schema, rollback without a backup becomes much harder.

API and Web versions drift apart

Even if the UI loads, behavior may still be inconsistent when bundle versions do not match.

docs.title: Backup and Restore
docs.text:
ScipionWeb disaster recovery requires backing up both the database and the runtime workspace.

A complete backup includes:

  1. PostgreSQL dump (metadata, users, settings, tags, sharing, protocol records)
  2. SCIPION_HOME backup (projects, logs, runtime config, deployed web bundle, .env)

Full recovery requires both parts

Backing up only PostgreSQL or only SCIPION_HOME is not sufficient for a reliable restore.

docs.title: PostgreSQL Database
docs.text:
The database stores application metadata such as:

  • Users
  • Project metadata
  • Protocol metadata
  • Sharing information
  • Settings
  • Tags

docs.title: SCIPION_HOME
docs.text:
The runtime workspace stores operational data such as:

  • projects/
  • logs/
  • config/
  • web/
  • .env

Keep backups separate

Store database dumps and filesystem archives as separate artifacts. This makes restores and validation easier.

docs.title: Backup Strategy Overview
docs.text:
Recommended baseline strategy:

  • Daily PostgreSQL backups
  • Weekly SCIPION_HOME filesystem backups
  • Off-site / external storage
  • Regular restore testing (do not skip this)
Backup Component Frequency Why It Matters PostgreSQL dump Daily Protects users, metadata, settings, tags SCIPION_HOME archive Weekly (or daily in active systems) Protects projects, logs, runtime config, .env Restore verification Scheduled Confirms backups are actually usable

docs.title: Plain SQL dump
docs.text: bash pg_dump -U scipion_user scipion_db > scipion_backup.sql

docs.title: Compressed dump
docs.text:
bash pg_dump -U scipion_user scipion_db | gzip > scipion_backup.sql.gz

Compressed backups

Compressed dumps are usually better for storage efficiency and transfer.

docs.title: Backup SCIPION_HOME
docs.text:
Create a compressed archive:

bash tar -czf scipion_home_backup.tar.gz scipion_home/

If your SCIPION_HOME lives elsewhere, use the absolute path:

bash tar -czf scipion_home_backup.tar.gz /opt/scipionweb/scipion_home/

docs.title: Full Backup Script Example
docs.text:
Minimal one-shot example:

bash pg_dump -U scipion_user scipion_db | gzip > db_backup.sql.gz tar -czf scipion_home_backup.tar.gz scipion_home/

Store backups outside the host

Keep copies in a secure external location (separate disk, backup server, object storage, etc.). Backups on the same machine are not enough for disaster recovery.

docs.title: Restore Workflow Overview
docs.text:
Recommended restore order:

  1. Restore database
  2. Restore SCIPION_HOME
  3. Validate .env and permissions
  4. Start services
  5. Verify /health and logs

Restore to staging first

When possible, test recovery in an isolated environment before touching production.

docs.title: Step 1: Create an empty database
docs.text: bash createdb -U scipion_user scipion_db

docs.title: Step 2: Restore from plain SQL dump
docs.text: bash psql -U scipion_user scipion_db < scipion_backup.sql

docs.title: Step 3: Restore from compressed dump (if applicable)
docs.text:
bash gunzip -c db_backup.sql.gz | psql -U scipion_user scipion_db

Existing database

If the target database already exists and contains data, ensure you are restoring into the correct target and expected state before executing restore commands.

docs.title: Restore SCIPION_HOME
docs.text:
Extract the archive:

bash tar -xzf scipion_home_backup.tar.gz

Ensure the service user can read/write the restored workspace:

bash chmod -R u+rw scipion_home

If using a dedicated service account, also fix ownership as needed:

bash chown -R youruser:yourgroup scipion_home

docs.title: Post-Restore Validation
docs.text:
After restoring both components:

  1. Verify .env exists and matches the intended deployment
  2. Confirm SECRET_KEY consistency
  3. Start services
  4. Check health endpoint
  5. Inspect logs

docs.title: Health check
docs.text:
bash curl http://localhost:8080/health

Expected:

json {"status":"ok"}

docs.title: Runtime checks
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi status ./scripts/scipionapi logs

docs.title: Disaster Recovery Checklist
docs.text:
Use this checklist during incidents:

  • [ ] Restore PostgreSQL dump
  • [ ] Restore SCIPION_HOME
  • [ ] Validate .env
  • [ ] Confirm SECRET_KEY matches expected deployment
  • [ ] Fix permissions/ownership
  • [ ] Start services
  • [ ] Verify /health
  • [ ] Verify frontend access
  • [ ] Inspect app.log / celery.log

docs.title: Production Notes
docs.text:
Backups without verification are not backups

Schedule regular restore drills (staging is enough) to validate credentials, file permissions, and actual recovery time.

Version discipline

Keep backup timestamps and version labels (API version, frontend bundle version, schema revision if relevant) to simplify rollback and incident response.

docs.title: Logs and PID Files
docs.text: ScipionAPI runtime management relies on log files and PID files for monitoring, debugging, and service lifecycle operations.

docs.title: Log Location
docs.text:
Runtime logs are stored under:

text SCIPION_HOME/logs/

docs.title: Typical Log Files
docs.text: text logs/ ├── app.log └── celery.log

docs.title: app.log
docs.text: Usually contains API startup messages, request handling logs, and error traces.

docs.title: celery.log
docs.text:
Usually contains worker startup messages, task execution logs, failures, and retries.

First place to look

If the UI or API appears healthy but tasks fail silently, check celery.log early.

docs.title: CLI shortcut
docs.text: bash ./scripts/scipionapi logs

docs.title: Manual live tail
docs.text: bash tail -f scipion_home/logs/app.log tail -f scipion_home/logs/celery.log

docs.title: PID Files
docs.text:
When using CLI runtime commands (start, stop, restart), ScipionAPI writes PID files under:

text .run/

Typical layout:

text .run/ ├── api.pid └── worker.pid

PID files help stop, restart, and inspect runtime services safely.

docs.title: Common pitfall: stale PID files
docs.text:
After an unexpected crash, PID files may remain even though the processes are gone.

Typical symptoms:

  • status reports a process that is not really alive
  • start refuses to launch because a PID file already exists
  • stop cannot act on the recorded PID

Always verify the process state before assuming the PID file is trustworthy.

docs.title: How to use logs well
docs.text:
A useful operational habit is to correlate three things together:

  1. the user action or failing workflow
  2. the API log around that time
  3. the worker log around that same time

This usually helps you decide whether the failure belongs to request handling, background execution, or environment state.

docs.title: Quick Ops Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] SCIPION_HOME/logs/ exists and is writable
  • [ ] app.log updates during API requests
  • [ ] celery.log updates during task execution
  • [ ] .run/ PID files match live processes
  • [ ] logs are reviewed regularly in production

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
This page summarizes practical security guidance for deploying ScipionWeb in production.

Shared responsibility

Security depends on both the application and the deployment environment (reverse proxy, firewall, service user permissions, secrets handling, and monitoring).

docs.title: Security Priorities (At a Glance)
docs.text:
• Use HTTPS
  • Protect SECRET_KEY
  • Restrict CORS
  • Keep PostgreSQL and Redis non-public
  • Enforce proper file permissions
  • Monitor logs and automate backups

docs.title: 1. Use HTTPS
docs.text:
Never expose the API over plain HTTP in production.

Recommended setup:

  • Reverse proxy (for example nginx)
  • TLS certificates (Let's Encrypt or enterprise CA)

Example public endpoints:

text https://yourdomain.com https://api.yourdomain.com

Why HTTPS is mandatory

HTTPS protects credentials, tokens, and session traffic from interception.

docs.title: 2. Protect SECRET_KEY
docs.text:
SECRET_KEY is used to sign JWT tokens.

It:

  • Must remain private
  • Must not be committed to version control
  • Should be rotated if compromised

Recommended practices:

  • Use a strong random value (32+ characters minimum)
  • Store it in .env with restricted permissions
  • Consider injecting it via systemd environment configuration for additional hardening

Compromise response

If SECRET_KEY is exposed, rotate it and assume existing tokens may be untrusted.

docs.title: 3. Restrict CORS
docs.text:
Avoid permissive production CORS policies such as:

python allow_origins=["*"]

Use explicit origins instead:

python allow_origins=["https://yourdomain.com"]

If you use separate frontend/API hosts, include only the required frontend origins.

docs.title: 4. Database Security (PostgreSQL)
docs.text:
Recommended controls:

  • Use strong passwords
  • Bind PostgreSQL to localhost when possible
  • Do not expose port 5432 publicly
  • Apply firewall rules
  • Limit remote access to trusted hosts only (if remote DB is required)

Defense in depth

Even if PostgreSQL requires credentials, network exposure should still be minimized.

docs.title: 5. Redis Security
docs.text:
Recommended controls:

  • Bind Redis to localhost
  • Do not expose Redis publicly
  • Consider authentication if Redis must be remote
  • Restrict network access with firewall rules

High-risk exposure

Public Redis exposure is a common operational mistake and can lead to severe compromise.

docs.title: 6. Firewall Configuration
docs.text:
Typical port policy:

  • 80 / 443 → public (if internet-facing)
  • 8080 → internal only (when reverse proxy is used)
  • 5432 (PostgreSQL) → not public
  • 6379 (Redis) → not public

Use host firewall rules and/or cloud security groups to enforce this.

docs.title: 7. JWT Security Practices
docs.text:
JWT security depends on both backend behavior and deployment choices.

Recommended:

  • Use HTTPS everywhere
  • Use short-lived access tokens
  • Consider refresh-token strategy
  • Invalidate access when users are disabled or permissions change (application-policy dependent)

docs.title: 8. File Permissions
docs.text:
Protect runtime configuration and data:

  • .env should be readable only by the service user
  • scipion_home should not be world-writable
  • Ensure correct ownership for the runtime user/service

Example:

bash chmod 600 scipion_home/.env chmod -R 750 scipion_home

Optional ownership fix:

bash chown -R youruser:yourgroup scipion_home

docs.title: 9. Production Hardening Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] HTTPS enabled
  • [ ] Reverse proxy configured
  • [ ] SECRET_KEY secured
  • [ ] CORS restricted to trusted origins
  • [ ] PostgreSQL not publicly exposed
  • [ ] Redis not publicly exposed
  • [ ] Runtime permissions hardened (.env, SCIPION_HOME)
  • [ ] Logs monitored
  • [ ] Backups automated and tested

docs.title: 10. Security Model Summary
docs.text:
ScipionWeb security relies on:

  • JWT authentication
  • Backend validation
  • Secure runtime configuration
  • Deployment hardening (network, proxy, permissions, monitoring)

Operational reality

Security is not a one-time setup. Revisit your configuration after upgrades, topology changes, or new public exposure.
