docs.title: Build API Bundle
docs.text: This document explains how to package the ScipionAPI backend into a distributable release bundle.

docs.title: Step 1 — Clean Workspace
docs.text:
From repo root:

bash git clean -xfd

Or manually ensure:

  • No logs
  • No .run/
  • No scipion_home/
  • No venvs

docs.title: Step 2 — Validate Production State
docs.text:
Ensure:

  • Migrations pass
  • Tests pass (if present)
  • Provision works on clean machine

docs.title: Step 3 — Exclude Runtime Artifacts
docs.text:
Make sure these are NOT included:

scipion_home/ .run/ .env __pycache__/

docs.title: Step 4 — Create Release Folder
docs.text: bash mkdir scipionapi-1.2.0 rsync -av --exclude scipion_home --exclude .run ./ scipionapi-1.2.0/

docs.title: Step 5 — Create ZIP
docs.text: bash zip -r scipionapi-1.2.0-linux-x86_64.zip scipionapi-1.2.0/

docs.title: Step 6 — Upload
docs.text:
Upload bundle to release server:

https://scipion.cnb.csic.es/downloads/scipion/scipionWeb/

docs.title: Optional: Checksum
docs.text: bash sha256sum scipionapi-1.2.0-linux-x86_64.zip > scipionapi-1.2.0.sha256

docs.title: Recommended Automation
docs.text:
Eventually automate with:

  • Makefile
  • GitHub Actions
  • CI pipeline

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Bundle extracts cleanly
  • README included
  • Provision works from extracted folder
  • No secrets present

docs.title: Build Web Bundle
docs.text: This document explains how to build and package the ScipionWeb frontend.

docs.title: Step 1 — Install Dependencies
docs.text: bash npm install

docs.title: Step 2 — Production Build
docs.text:
bash npm run build

This produces:

dist/

docs.title: Step 3 — Validate Dist
docs.text:
Ensure:

dist/index.html dist/assets/

Open locally:

bash npx serve dist

docs.title: Step 4 — Create Bundle
docs.text:
Option A — Zip dist directly:

bash zip -r scipionweb-1.2.0-dist.zip dist/

Option B — Versioned folder:

bash mkdir scipionweb-1.2.0 cp -r dist scipionweb-1.2.0/ zip -r scipionweb-1.2.0-dist.zip scipionweb-1.2.0/

docs.title: Step 5 — Upload
docs.text:
Upload to:

https://scipion.cnb.csic.es/downloads/scipion/scipionWeb/

docs.title: Runtime Configuration
docs.text:
The API installer injects:

dist/config.js

Which defines:

js window.__SCIPION_WEB_CONFIG__ = { apiBaseUrl: "/api" }

No rebuild required for different deployments.

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text: ✔ Same web build everywhere ✔ Runtime-configurable API URL ✔ CDN friendly ✔ Cacheable

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Web loads without API
  • API base URL injected correctly
  • SPA routing works

docs.title: Release Checklist
docs.text: Use this checklist before publishing any ScipionWeb release.

docs.title: API Bundle
docs.text:
• [ ] Clean working directory
  • [ ] Migrations apply cleanly
  • [ ] Provision works on a fresh machine
  • [ ] No runtime folders included
  • [ ] README updated
  • [ ] Version bumped

docs.title: Web Bundle
docs.text:
• [ ] Build succeeds
  • [ ] dist contains the expected assets
  • [ ] SPA routing works
  • [ ] No hardcoded API URLs
  • [ ] runtime config injection works as expected

docs.title: Integration Validation
docs.text:
• [ ] Download API bundle
  • [ ] Download Web bundle
  • [ ] Extract both
  • [ ] Run provision with --web-dist
  • [ ] Access UI in browser
  • [ ] Login works
  • [ ] Projects load

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• [ ] Install guide updated
  • [ ] Upgrade guide updated
  • [ ] Known issues documented
  • [ ] Support guidance still points to the correct public channels

docs.title: Release Artifacts
docs.text:
• [ ] API zip uploaded
  • [ ] Web zip uploaded
  • [ ] version announcement prepared

docs.title: Final Sanity Questions
docs.text:
Before tagging, confirm:

  • would a new user be able to install this version from the published docs?
  • would an existing user understand the upgrade path?
  • are the support channels ready for incoming questions or bug reports?

docs.title: Final Step
docs.text:
Tag the release:

bash git tag v1.2.0 git push origin v1.2.0

docs.title: Packaging Strategy
docs.text:
This section describes how ScipionWeb is packaged and released as installable bundles.

The goal is to provide a human-friendly installation experience:

  • No git cloning required
  • No manual dependency wiring
  • One-shot provisioning
  • Clean upgrades

docs.title: High-Level Concept
docs.text:
Each release consists of two artifacts:

Bundle Purpose ScipionAPI bundle Backend runtime + installer ScipionWeb bundle Pre-built frontend (Vite dist)

Users download, extract, provision, and run.

docs.title: API Bundle
docs.text: scipionapi-X.Y.Z/ ├── alembic/ ├── app/ ├── scipionapi_cli/ ├── scripts/ ├── requirements.txt ├── pyproject.toml └── README.md

docs.title: Web Bundle
docs.text: scipionweb-X.Y.Z/ └── dist/ ├── index.html ├── assets/ └── config.js (runtime injected)

docs.title: Why Two Bundles?
docs.text:
Separation enables:

  • Independent scaling
  • Remote API deployments
  • CDN web hosting
  • Easier upgrades

But also supports:

  • Integrated mode (API serves web)

docs.title: Supported Deployment Models
docs.text: Model Description Integrated API + Web on same host Separate API server + Web static host Hybrid API private + Web public

docs.title: Versioning Strategy
docs.text:
Recommended:

MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH

Example:

1.2.0

Where:

  • MAJOR: breaking schema/runtime
  • MINOR: features
  • PATCH: fixes

docs.title: Release Artifact Naming
docs.text: scipionapi-1.2.0-linux-x86_64.zip scipionweb-1.2.0-dist.zip

docs.title: Distribution Endpoint
docs.text:
Example:

https://scipion.cnb.csic.es/downloads/scipion/scipionWeb/

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text: ✔ No git required ✔ No manual Python env juggling ✔ Deterministic installs ✔ Reproducible runtime ✔ Human-friendly commands

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
ScipionWeb follows a bundle + provision deployment philosophy:

Download → Extract → Provision → Run

docs.title: Support Overview
docs.text:
Use this section when you need help with ScipionWeb, want to report a problem, or want to suggest an improvement.

The goal is to help you choose the right support path quickly.

docs.title: Choose the right path
docs.text: Situation Best place to start I do not know how to do something Ask a Question Something fails repeatedly Report a Bug I have an idea to improve the UI or workflow Suggest an Improvement I want to check recurring problems first Known Issues and Workarounds

docs.title: Official public support channels
docs.text:
ScipionWeb support is currently centered around the public GitHub repository:

  • ScipionWeb repository: fonsecareyna82/ScipionWeb
  • Discussions: Ask questions and discuss ideas
  • Issues: Open or review actionable reports
  • New issue chooser: Create a new report

docs.title: When to ask a question
docs.text:
Use this path when:

  • you are not sure whether behavior is expected
  • you need help with installation or runtime setup
  • you need help using a workflow in the web interface
  • you want clarification before opening a bug report

➡️ Open Ask a Question

docs.title: When to report a bug
docs.text:
Use this path when:

  • the behavior is reproducible
  • something clearly fails or regressed
  • you can describe steps, environment, and expected behavior
  • you have logs, screenshots, or error messages that help explain the problem

➡️ Open Report a Bug

docs.title: When to suggest an improvement
docs.text:
Use this path when:

  • the software is working but could work better
  • you want to propose a UX, workflow, or capability improvement
  • you want to discuss an idea before it becomes a task

➡️ Open Suggest an Improvement

docs.title: Before opening a new report
docs.text:
It is often useful to check:

  • Known Issues and Workarounds
  • existing GitHub Issues
  • existing GitHub Discussions

This reduces duplicate reports and may give you a workaround immediately.

docs.title: What good support depends on
docs.text:
Support becomes much faster and more useful when users provide:

  • the exact environment they are using
  • clear steps
  • the actual error message
  • relevant logs or screenshots
  • what they expected instead

docs.title: Recommended support model
docs.text:
For a public technical project, the most practical model is:

  • GitHub Discussions for questions, troubleshooting, and open-ended ideas
  • GitHub Issues for confirmed bugs and actionable feature requests

That keeps user support visible, searchable, and close to the real project.

docs.title: Ask a Question
docs.text:
Use this path when you need help understanding ScipionWeb behavior, installation, deployment, or usage.

A question is the right starting point when you are unsure whether the problem is:

  • an expected limitation
  • a configuration mistake
  • a known issue
  • a real software bug

docs.title: Best support channel
docs.text:
Use GitHub Discussions for questions, troubleshooting, and clarification:

➡️ Open ScipionWeb Discussions

That makes it easier to:

  • keep the conversation open
  • get feedback before opening a bug report
  • help future users who hit the same issue

docs.title: Good questions usually include
docs.text:
• ScipionWeb version or bundle version
  • ScipionAPI version if relevant
  • deployment mode (integrated, API-only, development)
  • operating system and relevant environment details
  • what you were trying to do
  • what actually happened
  • any visible error message
  • any relevant log excerpt or screenshot

docs.title: Before asking
docs.text:
Check these first:

  • Common Errors and Fixes
  • Backend Troubleshooting
  • Known Issues and Workarounds
  • ScipionWeb issue list

docs.title: Question template you can copy
docs.text:
```text Environment: ScipionWeb version: ScipionAPI version: Deployment mode: OS / runtime details:

What I was trying to do:

What happened instead:

Error message or logs:

What I already checked: ```

docs.title: When not to use this path
docs.text: If the behavior is clearly reproducible and you already have solid reproduction steps, use Report a Bug instead.

docs.title: Known Issues and Workarounds
docs.text:
Use this page to document recurring problems that already have useful checks or workarounds.

This page is intentionally curated. GitHub Issues and Discussions may contain newer raw activity, but this page should summarize the patterns that appear often enough to help users recover faster.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• A plugin installation, protocol execution, or background action is submitted successfully.
  • The task remains in PENDING for a long time.
  • The UI does not show task progress.

docs.title: Common causes
docs.text:
• The Celery worker is not running.
  • Redis is not reachable from the API or worker.
  • The API and worker are using different .env files or different broker URLs.
  • The worker started without the expected SCIPION_HOME.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
bash ./scripts/scipionapi status ./scripts/scipionapi logs redis-cli ping

Expected Redis response:

text PONG

docs.title: Workaround
docs.text:
Restart the runtime after verifying that SCIPION_HOME, CELERY_BROKER_URL, and CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND are consistent:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi restart

docs.title: Redis reports no nodes replied within time constraint
Symptoms
docs.text:
• Celery status commands fail or time out.
  • Background tasks do not progress.
  • Logs mention that no Redis nodes replied within the expected time.

docs.title: Common causes
docs.text:
• Redis is stopped or overloaded.
  • The configured Redis URL points to the wrong host, port, or database.
  • Firewall or network restrictions block access in a distributed deployment.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text: bash sudo systemctl status redis-server redis-cli ping

docs.title: Workaround
docs.text:
Start or restart Redis, then restart ScipionAPI services:

bash sudo systemctl restart redis-server ./scripts/scipionapi restart

docs.title: SCIPION_HOME is not available in the worker
Symptoms
docs.text:
• The API starts, but Celery tasks fail immediately.
  • Worker logs mention missing runtime configuration or missing SCIPION_HOME.
  • Tasks behave differently when run from a shell and from the worker.

docs.title: Common causes
docs.text:
• The worker was started from a shell that did not export SCIPION_HOME.
  • The worker process is not loading SCIPION_HOME/.env.
  • API and worker were started through different mechanisms.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text: bash echo "$SCIPION_HOME" test -f "$SCIPION_HOME/.env" && echo ".env found" ./scripts/scipionapi logs

docs.title: Workaround
docs.text:
Start API and worker through the same runtime command so both processes share the same configuration source:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi restart

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Backend startup or Celery worker startup fails.
  • Logs show an import error involving pyworkflow or pyworkflow.project.manager.

docs.title: Common causes
docs.text:
• The wrong Conda environment is active.
  • Scipion dependencies were not installed in the environment used by the process.
  • The worker is started outside the wrapper script or expected environment.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text: bash conda activate scipion4Web python -c "import pyworkflow; print(pyworkflow.__file__)"

docs.title: Workaround
docs.text:
Activate the expected environment and restart using the wrapper script from the ScipionAPI bundle directory:

bash conda activate scipion4Web ./scripts/scipionapi restart

docs.title: Browser CORS errors between frontend and backend
Symptoms
docs.text:
• Requests work with curl but fail in the browser.
  • Browser DevTools show CORS or preflight errors.
  • This often appears in development mode with the Web UI on localhost:5173 and the API on localhost:8080.

docs.title: Common causes
docs.text:
• The frontend origin is not allowed by the backend.
  • The configured API URL does not match the real browser origin.
  • A reverse proxy changes scheme, host, or port unexpectedly.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
• Confirm the exact frontend URL shown in the browser.
  • Confirm the backend CORS configuration includes that exact origin.
  • Check failed requests in browser DevTools.

docs.title: Workaround
docs.text: Add the exact frontend origin to the backend CORS configuration and restart the API.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Migration commands fail with a message similar to Can't locate revision identified by ....
  • Upgrade or install fails while applying database migrations.

docs.title: Common causes
docs.text:
• The database migration history points to a revision that is not present in the current code checkout or bundle.
  • The wrong database is being targeted.
  • A partially applied migration state was left behind after a failed test or upgrade.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
bash alembic current alembic heads

Also confirm that DATABASE_URL points to the intended database.

docs.title: Workaround
docs.text:
For test databases, the simplest fix is often to recreate the database and apply migrations from a clean state.

For real deployments, do not edit migration history blindly. Back up the database first, inspect the missing revision, and decide whether a controlled stamp or migration repair is appropriate.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• API startup fails with an address-in-use error.
  • provision or start appears to fail immediately.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text: bash lsof -i :8080

docs.title: Workaround
docs.text:
Stop the process using the port, or configure ScipionAPI to use a different API port.

Use forceful termination only as a last resort after confirming the process is safe to stop.

docs.title: When to add new entries
docs.text:
Add a new entry here when the same issue appears repeatedly in development, installation, user support, or GitHub discussions.

A useful entry should include symptoms, likely causes, checks, and a workaround.

docs.title: Report a Bug
docs.text:
Use this path when the behavior is clearly wrong, reproducible, or regressed.

A bug report is most useful when it helps another person reproduce the problem quickly.

docs.title: Best support channel
docs.text:
Use GitHub Issues for reproducible bugs:

➡️ Open the issue chooser

That gives you:

  • one report per problem
  • labels and prioritization
  • clear triage workflow
  • easier linking to fixes, commits, and releases

docs.title: A strong bug report includes
docs.text:
• exact environment and version
  • clear reproduction steps
  • expected behavior
  • actual behavior
  • relevant logs, traceback, or screenshots
  • whether the problem is new or was working before

docs.title: Before opening a bug report
docs.text:
Check whether the problem may already be explained by:

  • configuration mismatch
  • stale browser session state
  • environment-specific dependency issues
  • a known issue already documented or discussed

See also:

  • Ask a Question
  • Known Issues and Workarounds
  • ScipionWeb issue list

docs.title: Bug report template you can copy
docs.text:
```text Environment: ScipionWeb version: ScipionAPI version: Deployment mode: OS / runtime details:

Steps to reproduce: 1. 2. 3.

Expected behavior:

Actual behavior:

Logs / traceback / screenshots:

Regression?: Did this work before? ```

docs.title: When not to use this path
docs.text: If you are still unsure whether the behavior is a bug at all, start with Ask a Question in Discussions.

docs.title: Suggest an Improvement
docs.text:
Use this path when the software is working, but you think the workflow, UI, behavior, or capabilities could be improved.

Improvement discussions are valuable because they help shape the roadmap before an idea becomes a concrete implementation task.

docs.title: Best support channel
docs.text:
Use GitHub Discussions first for open-ended ideas:

➡️ Open ScipionWeb Discussions

That helps clarify:

  • whether the idea solves a real recurring problem
  • whether other users want the same thing
  • whether the proposal should later become a feature request issue

When an improvement becomes concrete and actionable, it can then move into GitHub Issues.

docs.title: Good improvement proposals usually explain
docs.text:
• what part of ScipionWeb feels limited or awkward
  • who is affected by the problem
  • what the current workaround is, if any
  • what better outcome you would like to achieve
  • whether the idea is about UX, performance, reliability, or functionality

docs.title: Improvement proposal template you can copy
docs.text:
```text Area affected: Who is affected:

Current behavior:

Why this is a problem:

Suggested improvement:

Workaround today:

Additional context: ```

docs.title: When to turn an idea into an issue
docs.text: Open a GitHub Issue when the proposal becomes specific enough to track as planned work.

docs.title: User Guide Overview
docs.text:
This section explains how to use ScipionWeb as an application once the platform is already running.

It is written for users who already have access to a working instance and need guidance on the most common workflows inside the web interface.

docs.title: What this section is for
docs.text:
Use the User Guide when you want to understand:

  • how to start a session and verify that the application is working correctly
  • how to work safely with projects
  • how to configure and launch protocols
  • how to inspect outputs and viewers
  • how to collaborate with teammates
  • how to approach settings and plugins without guessing

docs.title: Recommended reading order
docs.text:
1. First Login and Session Basics
  2. Projects
  3. Protocol Execution
  4. Outputs and Viewers
  5. Sharing and Collaboration
  6. Settings
  7. Plugins
  8. Working Efficiently

docs.title: New user
docs.text:
1. First Login and Session Basics
  2. Projects
  3. Protocol Execution

docs.title: User validating results
docs.text:
1. Projects
  2. Outputs and Viewers

docs.title: User collaborating with teammates
docs.text:
1. Projects
  2. Sharing and Collaboration

docs.title: User configuring the workspace
docs.text:
1. Settings
  2. Plugins
  3. Working Efficiently

docs.title: Scope note
docs.text:
What this section does not replace

This section complements, but does not replace, the technical documentation for:

  • Installation
  • Configuration
  • Operations
  • Backend Troubleshooting

docs.title: Important expectation
docs.text: The exact visual layout of the interface can evolve over time. Even when labels or menus move slightly, the workflow principles described here should still help you understand what to verify before and after each action.

docs.title: First Login and Session Basics
docs.text:
Use this page for the first actions after opening ScipionWeb in the browser.

Start from the login page and sign in with your user credentials.

ScipionWeb login page used to start a user session.

After signing in, ScipionWeb opens the dashboard. This is the first place to check that the application loaded correctly and that your session is active.

Dashboard page shown after a successful login.

From the dashboard, open the Projects area to confirm that protected API requests and project data load correctly.

The Projects page is a useful follow-up check because it confirms that session state and protected API requests are working.

docs.title: Before you troubleshoot login
docs.text:
Confirm these basics first:

  • you are opening the intended environment
  • the backend instance is actually reachable
  • your browser is not reusing stale session state from another instance
  • you are not mixing tabs from different environments

docs.title: After login, verify that
docs.text:
• the dashboard loads correctly
  • the main navigation loads correctly
  • your user identity appears as expected
  • the projects area opens without errors
  • requests complete without browser auth or CORS failures
  • protected data loads consistently after refresh

If the interface appears but project-related data never loads, the problem is often session state or backend/API connectivity rather than visual rendering.

docs.title: Invalid credentials
docs.text: Check that you are using the correct username, password, and environment.

docs.title: Login works but protected pages fail
docs.text:
Possible causes include:

  • stale token stored in the browser
  • backend SECRET_KEY changed
  • API base URL mismatch
  • session state was created against another environment

docs.title: The page loads but actions fail
docs.text: Open browser DevTools and inspect failed network requests under /api.

docs.title: Useful recovery actions
docs.text:
Try these steps in order:

  1. log out and log in again
  2. refresh the page after a clean sign-in
  3. clear stale browser storage if the instance configuration changed recently
  4. retry from a private browser window to isolate local-state issues

Private window test

A private window is often the fastest way to check whether the issue is caused by cached tokens or stale local browser state.

docs.title: Good habits for multi-environment work
docs.text:
If you regularly switch between local, staging, and production:

  • keep those environments in clearly named bookmarks
  • avoid leaving old tabs open for long periods
  • confirm the URL before changing protected data
  • do not assume a saved session belongs to the environment you intend to use now

docs.title: When to ask an administrator for help
docs.text:
Ask for help when:

  • the same issue reproduces across browsers or machines
  • your account should exist but access is denied
  • the instance is reachable but protected data never loads
  • the backend appears healthy but login still fails consistently

docs.title: Outputs and Viewers
docs.text: This page explains how to inspect protocol results in ScipionWeb and how to decide whether a workflow can continue safely.

docs.title: Typical output workflow
docs.text:
After a protocol finishes, you will usually:

  1. locate the generated outputs in the protocol card, protocol details, or output area
  2. confirm that the protocol finished successfully
  3. open the relevant preview or viewer
  4. inspect the data in the context of the project and protocol history
  5. decide whether a downstream protocol can use that output

docs.title: Open an output
docs.text:
1. Open the project that contains the protocol run.
  2. Locate the protocol that produced the result.
  3. Check that the protocol status is finished or otherwise suitable for inspection.
  4. Locate the output you want to review.
  5. Open the preview or viewer action associated with that output.
  6. Confirm that the viewer content matches the expected output type.

Outputs can be opened from the project context using the available viewer actions.

Before assuming a viewer is broken, confirm that:

  • the protocol completed successfully
  • the selected output belongs to the expected run
  • the viewer matches the output type
  • browser and backend requests are succeeding

docs.title: Common viewer expectations
docs.text:
Different output types may open different viewers or previews.

Typical examples include:

  • metadata tables or galleries
  • volume viewers
  • coordinate viewers
  • tilt-series viewers
  • CTF tomography viewers
  • generic file previews

Metadata outputs can be inspected in a table-oriented viewer.

The exact viewer depends on the output class and the backend data available for that output.

docs.title: While inspecting results
docs.text:
Review outputs with these questions in mind:

  • is this the expected result for the current workflow step?
  • does the output belong to the intended protocol execution?
  • are there obvious signs that the run failed or produced incomplete data?
  • do follow-up protocols now have the inputs they need?

Outputs are most useful when interpreted as part of a workflow, not as isolated files detached from the protocol history.

docs.title: Use outputs as downstream inputs
docs.text:
Before selecting an output as input for another protocol:

  1. verify that the output belongs to the intended project and protocol run
  2. inspect it if the output type has a useful viewer
  3. confirm that the output type is compatible with the downstream protocol parameter
  4. avoid selecting similarly named outputs from older runs by mistake

This is especially important in projects with repeated runs, duplicated protocols, or several similar outputs.

docs.title: If a viewer does not open
docs.text:
Check the following first:

  • failed browser network requests
  • missing backend viewer data
  • stale frontend state after route changes
  • incomplete protocol execution
  • whether the selected output still corresponds to the visible protocol state

Useful recovery steps:

  1. refresh the project view
  2. reopen the protocol details
  3. try the viewer again
  4. inspect browser network errors
  5. check API logs if the viewer request fails server-side

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
When possible:

  • review outputs soon after execution finishes
  • compare what you see with what the workflow stage should produce
  • confirm that downstream steps are using the intended outputs
  • avoid relying only on memory when multiple similar runs exist in the same project

docs.title: Plugins
docs.text:
Plugins extend Scipion capabilities and may expose actions both in the backend and in the web interface.

Plugins page where available and installed plugins can be reviewed.

docs.title: Typical plugin-related tasks
docs.text:
In normal use, you may need to:

  • review available plugins
  • check whether a plugin is already installed
  • start an installation or removal flow
  • monitor plugin-related task progress
  • confirm that the UI reflects the new plugin state

docs.title: What to verify before changing plugin state
docs.text:
• you are working in the intended environment
  • the backend and worker are running correctly
  • Redis and Celery are healthy
  • the plugin action is allowed for your role or workflow
  • you understand whether the change affects only your current workflow or the whole instance

docs.title: During plugin operations
docs.text:
Pay attention to:

  • task status changes
  • installation or removal logs
  • whether the plugin becomes available only after backend reload or restart
  • whether the final UI state matches the reported backend result

Environment awareness

Plugin actions are especially easy to misread when local, staging, and production environments do not all have the same plugin state.

docs.title: Common plugin pitfalls
docs.text:
Problems often appear when:

  • the UI shows stale plugin state after a change
  • the worker is unhealthy even though the API is reachable
  • environment-specific dependencies break an installation flow
  • a user assumes a plugin is globally available because it was available elsewhere

docs.title: If a plugin operation gets stuck
docs.text:
Check:

  • worker logs
  • Redis availability
  • task status endpoint responses
  • whether the plugin introduced environment-specific dependency issues
  • whether the final state requires a refresh, restart, or explicit reload of backend-managed data

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
Projects are the main workspace unit in ScipionWeb. They group protocols, outputs, logs, and related analysis context.

Use this page when you need to create, open, rename, review, or share a project safely.

Projects page with project cards and project-level actions.

docs.title: Typical project workflow
docs.text:
A common project-oriented flow looks like this:

  1. open the Projects page
  2. create or open the intended project
  3. confirm the project identity and scope
  4. review visible protocols and outputs
  5. perform the next workflow action from the correct project context
  6. share the project if collaboration is needed

docs.title: Create a project
docs.text:
1. Open the Projects page.
  2. Select New Project.
  3. Enter a short but descriptive project name.
  4. Add a description if your team uses descriptions to document project purpose.
  5. Confirm the creation action.
  6. Open the new project and verify that the project page loads correctly.

New project dialog used to create a project workspace.

After creating a project, confirm:

  • the project appears in the project list
  • the name is easy to distinguish from similar projects
  • the project opens without API or authentication errors

docs.title: Open an existing project
docs.text:
1. Open the Projects page.
  2. Locate the intended project card or list entry.
  3. Check the project name, description, thumbnail, or recent status before opening it.
  4. Open the project.
  5. Review the visible workflow before launching or editing anything.

Project workflow view where protocols, status, and workflow context are reviewed.

Verify these basics first:

  • project name and description match the expected workflow
  • visible protocols correspond to the intended dataset or experiment
  • current status and recent outputs look reasonable
  • you are not accidentally working in a similarly named project

This matters because many later actions depend on being in the right project context before launching, reviewing, or sharing anything.

docs.title: Rename a project
docs.text:
Use rename when the project remains relevant but its label needs clarification.

  1. Open the project actions menu.
  2. Select the rename action.
  3. Enter the new project name.
  4. Confirm the change.
  5. Refresh or revisit the project list if the old name still appears temporarily.

Before renaming, confirm:

  • no teammate is actively depending on the previous name
  • the new name follows your team convention
  • the rename is being applied in the intended environment

docs.title: Share a project
docs.text:
1. Open the project actions menu.
  2. Select the share action.
  3. Choose the target user or collaborator according to the available sharing UI.
  4. Confirm the sharing action.
  5. Ask the collaborator to refresh their project list if the project does not appear immediately.

Shared work needs coordination

In collaborative environments, project-level actions should be treated as workflow changes, not just cosmetic edits.

docs.title: Project hygiene recommendations
docs.text:
• keep names consistent
  • avoid near-duplicate project labels
  • review old projects deliberately instead of letting them accumulate without context
  • document collaboration expectations when the same project is shared by several users
  • prefer clarity over short-lived convenience in naming and organization

docs.title: If a project action fails
docs.text:
Check:

  • whether you are still authenticated
  • whether the project list refreshes correctly
  • whether browser requests are failing with auth or CORS errors
  • whether the backend API returns validation or permission errors
  • whether you are looking at stale UI state after a route change

Useful recovery steps:

  1. refresh the project list
  2. log out and log in again if authentication looks stale
  3. inspect failed browser network requests
  4. check API logs if the backend returns an error

docs.title: Protocol Execution
docs.text: This page describes the typical protocol flow in ScipionWeb: configure parameters, save intentionally, launch execution, monitor progress, and inspect outputs.

docs.title: Common workflow
docs.text:
A standard protocol workflow usually looks like this:

  1. open the relevant project
  2. choose the protocol to configure
  3. review required parameters and inputs
  4. save configuration changes intentionally
  5. launch the protocol
  6. monitor execution state and logs
  7. inspect outputs when execution finishes

docs.title: Open and configure a protocol
docs.text:
1. Open the intended project.
  2. Locate the protocol in the workflow view.
  3. Open the protocol form or details panel.
  4. Review required parameters first.
  5. Select inputs deliberately, especially pointer-like parameters that refer to outputs from previous protocols.
  6. Review optional parameters only after the required inputs are correct.

Protocol form showing configurable parameters and input fields.

Before changing values, confirm that you are editing the intended protocol run and not a similarly named one.

docs.title: Save before launching
docs.text:
If the form separates saving from execution, treat Save and Execute as different actions:

  1. Change parameter values.
  2. Save the protocol configuration.
  3. Confirm that validation errors, if any, are resolved.
  4. Launch only after the saved values represent the run you want.

Saved configuration vs execution

A protocol can be configured without being launched. Likewise, visible outputs may belong to a previous execution if a new launch has not finished yet.

docs.title: Before launch
docs.text:
Confirm these points first:

  • the project context is the correct one
  • required inputs are available
  • parameter values were reviewed deliberately
  • previous protocol state does not conflict with the next run
  • you understand whether you are configuring, saving, or launching

A careful pre-launch check is often the difference between a clean workflow and a confusing rerun later.

docs.title: Launch and monitor execution
docs.text:
1. Start execution from the protocol action or form.
  2. Watch the protocol status.
  3. Open logs when available.
  4. Keep an eye on progress indicators and error messages.
  5. Refresh the project view if status appears stale.

Protocol logs help users confirm what happened during execution.

During execution, pay attention to:

  • status changes
  • progress indicators
  • visible log output
  • whether the workflow remains responsive in the browser

If the UI state does not update, refresh the project view and confirm the backend requests are succeeding.

docs.title: After execution
docs.text:
Once the protocol finishes, review:

  • final status
  • generated outputs
  • whether the expected viewer or output action is available
  • whether another downstream step is now unlocked
  • whether the result matches what the workflow stage should have produced

A good habit is to inspect outputs before launching dependent downstream protocols.

docs.title: Common sources of confusion
docs.text:
Users often confuse these states:

  • parameters changed but not saved
  • configuration saved but launch not triggered yet
  • execution started but the worker has not processed the task yet
  • execution finished but outputs not reviewed yet
  • visible outputs belong to an earlier run rather than the latest one

Whenever something feels inconsistent, go back to the project context and verify the full protocol lifecycle again.

docs.title: If execution does not behave as expected
docs.text:
Check:

  • browser network errors
  • backend and worker logs
  • Redis and Celery health
  • whether the protocol parameters were saved correctly before launch
  • whether you are looking at the correct protocol run and project state

Useful checks for administrators or developers:

bash ./scripts/scipionapi status ./scripts/scipionapi logs redis-cli ping

If tasks remain pending, see Known Issues and Workarounds.

docs.title: Settings
docs.text:
The Settings area centralizes configuration that affects your account, instance behavior, or shared UI conventions.

Settings page with user, instance, and shared configuration areas.

docs.title: What you may find in Settings
docs.text:
Depending on your role and deployment, the Settings area can include:

  • user preferences
  • instance-level configuration views
  • tag management or shared metadata helpers
  • advanced configuration panels intended for administrators

docs.title: Before changing settings
docs.text:
Confirm the scope of the change:

  • does it affect only your user session?
  • does it affect the whole instance?
  • does it affect shared project organization?
  • is the change reversible if the result is not what you expected?

Settings are easier to manage safely when you understand whether they are personal, shared, or administrative.

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
• change one thing at a time
  • verify the result immediately after saving
  • avoid changing instance-level configuration unless you understand the impact
  • document shared conventions when settings affect multiple users
  • prefer clarity and consistency over ad-hoc changes that only one person understands

docs.title: Common settings mistakes
docs.text:
Users often run into trouble when they:

  • assume a setting is personal when it is actually shared
  • change multiple things before validating any of them
  • forget to refresh affected views after saving
  • test in the wrong environment and misread the result

docs.title: If a settings change does not persist
docs.text:
Check:

  • whether the save request succeeded
  • whether the page reloaded stale data
  • whether your role has permission to change that section
  • whether browser or API errors appeared during save
  • whether another source of configuration is overriding the expected value

docs.title: Sharing and Collaboration
docs.text:
Sharing allows multiple users to work with the same project context.

Sharing dialog used to grant project access to collaborators.

docs.title: Typical sharing workflow
docs.text:
A common collaboration flow usually includes:

  1. confirming the correct project is selected
  2. granting access to the intended teammate
  3. verifying that the collaborator can open the project
  4. keeping naming and ownership expectations clear

docs.title: Before sharing a project
docs.text:
Check these basics first:

  • you are in the intended environment
  • the project is really the one meant for collaboration
  • the target user exists in the same instance
  • your team agrees on how project changes will be coordinated

docs.title: Good collaboration practices
docs.text:
• share only the projects that actually need collaboration
  • keep project names stable and descriptive
  • avoid ambiguity about who is responsible for operational changes
  • communicate before high-impact changes in shared projects
  • make it clear which outputs or workflow stages are already validated

Shared context matters

Collaboration works better when teammates do not need to guess which project state is considered current or trustworthy.

docs.title: Common collaboration pitfalls
docs.text:
Problems often appear when:

  • a similarly named project is shared by mistake
  • two users assume different workflow stages are current
  • high-impact actions happen without coordination
  • one user is working in a different environment than the others

docs.title: If sharing does not work as expected
docs.text:
Check:

  • whether the intended user exists in the instance
  • whether the share action completed successfully
  • whether the collaborator is logged into the correct environment
  • whether browser requests return auth or permission errors
  • whether stale session state is hiding recent permission changes

docs.title: Working Efficiently
docs.text: This page collects practical habits that make day-to-day work in ScipionWeb more reliable and easier to understand.

docs.title: 1. Confirm context before acting
docs.text:
Before launching, sharing, renaming, or reviewing results, verify:

  • the environment you are using
  • the project you are in
  • the protocol or output you are looking at
  • whether the visible state belongs to the latest workflow step

A large fraction of user confusion comes from acting in the wrong context rather than from a broken feature.

docs.title: 2. Prefer clear naming over fast naming
docs.text:
Use names that help you and your teammates answer basic questions quickly:

  • what is this project about?
  • which dataset or experiment does it represent?
  • is this the current workflow or an older attempt?

Good names reduce mistakes later in protocol execution, sharing, and output review.

docs.title: 3. Treat save and launch as different moments
docs.text:
A good mental model is:

  • configure carefully
  • save intentionally
  • launch only when the saved state is the one you want

This helps avoid confusion about whether a protocol was merely edited or actually executed.

docs.title: 4. Review outputs early
docs.text:
Do not wait too long to inspect important outputs.

Early review helps you detect:

  • incorrect input selection
  • unexpected parameter choices
  • runs that completed but produced the wrong kind of result
  • downstream steps that should not continue yet

docs.title: 5. Coordinate shared work explicitly
docs.text:
In collaborative environments:

  • communicate before high-impact project actions
  • make it clear which project state is current
  • avoid silent assumptions about who validated which outputs
  • keep naming and ownership conventions stable

docs.title: 6. Use the browser as a debugging surface
docs.text:
When something feels inconsistent, inspect:

  • browser network requests
  • failed API responses
  • visible route or reload behavior
  • whether the page is showing stale state

You do not need to be a backend developer to benefit from checking the browser first.

docs.title: 7. Recheck before assuming the system is wrong
docs.text:
Before concluding that something failed, quickly verify:

  • session is still valid
  • the correct environment is open
  • the right project is selected
  • the expected protocol run is visible
  • the output belongs to the workflow step you think it does

This habit saves a surprising amount of time.
